results.question: Did Russell's covering stop on the lap after Verstappen in Canada 2025 successfully execute the over-cut, given Verstappen's out-lap pace versus Russell's in-lap?
results.author_note: Motorsport.com Canadian GP: Russell pitted one lap later, emerged ahead, covering off the undercut threat.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Russell's covering stop one lap after Verstappen in Canada 2025 successfully executed the overcut. Verstappen pitted on lap 11 (in-lap: 76.991s, out-lap: 93.11s) and emerged in P9, while Russell pitted on lap 12 (in-lap: 76.715s, out-lap: 92.351s) and came out in P7 — ahead of Verstappen. Russell's in-lap was marginally faster (0.276s) and his out-lap was also quicker (0.759s), meaning he gained track position through both phases of the stop sequence.
results.answerReasoning: The single returned row shows Verstappen's out-lap position as P9 and Russell's as P7 after their respective stops, confirming the overcut succeeded. Russell's in-lap (76.715s) was slightly faster than Verstappen's (76.991s), and his out-lap (92.351s) was also faster than Verstappen's (93.11s), supporting the position gain. The query's CASE statement explicitly labels this 'Overcut SUCCESS — Russell ahead after stops.'
results.generationNotes: The question asks whether Russell's covering stop (one lap after Verstappen) successfully executed an over-cut in Canada 2025 (session_key=9963). The approach: (1) Pull the first pit stop for both drivers (Verstappen #1, Russell #63) from core.stint_summary — the out_lap of stint 2 is the first lap after the stop, and in_lap = out_lap - 1. (2) Retrieve lap times and positions for Verstappen's out-lap (the lap Russell was still out on track, building the over-cut gap) and Russell's out-lap (the lap after Russell's stop). (3) Compare positions after each driver's out-lap to determine if Russell emerged ahead. (4) Supplement with analytics.undercut_overcut_history for Russell's heuristic overcut success count as a cross-check. The CASE statement provides a plain-language verdict on whether the over-cut succeeded. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9963)

results.sql:
WITH pit_stops AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    team_name,
    stint_number,
    lap_start AS out_lap,
    lap_start - 1 AS in_lap,
    compound_name,
    tyre_age_at_start
  FROM core.stint_summary
  WHERE session_key = 9963
    AND driver_number IN (1, 63)
    AND stint_number > 1
),
verstappen_stop AS (
  SELECT * FROM pit_stops WHERE driver_number = 1
  ORDER BY stint_number ASC
  LIMIT 1
),
russell_stop AS (
  SELECT * FROM pit_stops WHERE driver_number = 63
  ORDER BY stint_number ASC
  LIMIT 1
),
key_laps AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.team_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_duration,
    le.is_pit_lap,
    le.is_pit_out_lap,
    le.position_end_of_lap,
    le.compound_name,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.is_valid
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 9963
    AND le.driver_number IN (1, 63)
),
stop_context AS (
  SELECT
    v.driver_number        AS v_driver_number,
    v.driver_name          AS v_driver_name,
    v.in_lap               AS v_in_lap,
    v.out_lap              AS v_out_lap,
    r.driver_number        AS r_driver_number,
    r.driver_name          AS r_driver_name,
    r.in_lap               AS r_in_lap,
    r.out_lap              AS r_out_lap,
    r.out_lap - v.out_lap  AS out_lap_gap
  FROM verstappen_stop v
  CROSS JOIN russell_stop r
),
v_in_lap_data AS (
  SELECT kl.lap_number, kl.lap_duration, kl.position_end_of_lap
  FROM key_laps kl
  JOIN stop_context sc ON kl.driver_number = sc.v_driver_number AND kl.lap_number = sc.v_in_lap
),
v_out_lap_data AS (
  SELECT kl.lap_number, kl.lap_duration, kl.position_end_of_lap
  FROM key_laps kl
  JOIN stop_context sc ON kl.driver_number = sc.v_driver_number AND kl.lap_number = sc.v_out_lap
),
r_in_lap_data AS (
  SELECT kl.lap_number, kl.lap_duration, kl.position_end_of_lap
  FROM key_laps kl
  JOIN stop_context sc ON kl.driver_number = sc.r_driver_number AND kl.lap_number = sc.r_in_lap
),
r_out_lap_data AS (
  SELECT kl.lap_number, kl.lap_duration, kl.position_end_of_lap
  FROM key_laps kl
  JOIN stop_context sc ON kl.driver_number = sc.r_driver_number AND kl.lap_number = sc.r_out_lap
),
overcut_history AS (
  SELECT
    uoh.driver_number,
    uoh.driver_name,
    uoh.overcut_success_count,
    uoh.undercut_success_count,
    uoh.neutral_stop_count,
    uoh.total_stops
  FROM analytics.undercut_overcut_history uoh
  WHERE uoh.session_key = 9963
    AND uoh.driver_number = 63
)
SELECT
  sc.v_driver_name                          AS verstappen_name,
  sc.v_in_lap                               AS verstappen_in_lap,
  sc.v_out_lap                              AS verstappen_out_lap,
  v_in.lap_duration                         AS verstappen_in_lap_time_s,
  v_in.position_end_of_lap                  AS verstappen_pos_after_in_lap,
  v_out.lap_duration                        AS verstappen_out_lap_time_s,
  v_out.position_end_of_lap                 AS verstappen_pos_after_out_lap,
  sc.r_driver_name                          AS russell_name,
  sc.r_in_lap                               AS russell_in_lap,
  sc.r_out_lap                              AS russell_out_lap,
  r_in.lap_duration                         AS russell_in_lap_time_s,
  r_in.position_end_of_lap                  AS russell_pos_after_in_lap,
  r_out.lap_duration                        AS russell_out_lap_time_s,
  r_out.position_end_of_lap                 AS russell_pos_after_out_lap,
  sc.out_lap_gap                            AS russell_pitted_laps_after_verstappen,
  CASE
    WHEN r_out.position_end_of_lap < v_out.position_end_of_lap THEN 'Overcut SUCCESS — Russell ahead after stops'
    WHEN r_out.position_end_of_lap = v_out.position_end_of_lap THEN 'Neutral — same position after stops'
    ELSE 'Overcut FAILED — Russell behind after stops'
  END                                       AS overcut_outcome,
  oh.overcut_success_count                  AS russell_season_overcut_successes,
  oh.undercut_success_count                 AS russell_season_undercut_successes,
  oh.total_stops                            AS russell_season_total_stops
FROM stop_context sc
LEFT JOIN v_in_lap_data  v_in  ON TRUE
LEFT JOIN v_out_lap_data v_out ON TRUE
LEFT JOIN r_in_lap_data  r_in  ON TRUE
LEFT JOIN r_out_lap_data r_out ON TRUE
LEFT JOIN overcut_history oh   ON TRUE
LIMIT 1

results.question: What was the average stationary time for Ferrari's pit crew at the 2025 Chinese GP versus the field median?

results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At the 2025 Chinese GP, Ferrari's pit crew averaged 22.284 seconds of stationary time across 3 stops (Hamilton: 22.388s on lap 13, Leclerc: 22.100s on lap 15, Hamilton: 22.364s on lap 37), which was 0.437 seconds faster than the field median of 22.721 seconds. The field average across all 25 stops was 23.350 seconds, making Ferrari one of the quicker pit crews at this race.
results.answerReasoning: The query returns Ferrari's average pit duration (22.284s) and the field median (22.721s) for session_key 9998 (2025 Chinese GP). The delta of -0.437s confirms Ferrari was faster than the median. Individual stop details are provided in the stop_detail field.
results.generationNotes: The question asks for Ferrari's average pit crew stationary time versus the field median at the 2025 Chinese GP (session_key=9998). I use raw.pit for pit_duration (stationary time), join core.session_drivers to identify Ferrari drivers, then compute Ferrari's avg/best/worst and the field-wide median and average. A filter of pit_duration between 0 and 60 seconds removes outliers (e.g. drive-throughs or data artifacts). The delta vs field median is also surfaced. Driver numbers 1 and 16 are the resolved Ferrari drivers but I filter by team_name to be robust. The stop_detail column lists each individual stop for transparency. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9998)

previewRows.stop_detail: Lewis HAMILTON (Lap 13: 22.388s), Charles LECLERC (Lap 15: 22.100s), Lewis HAMILTON (Lap 37: 22.364s)

results.sql:
WITH ferrari_pit AS (
    SELECT
        sd.driver_number,
        sd.full_name,
        sd.team_name,
        p.date,
        p.pit_duration,
        p.lap_number
    FROM raw.pit p
    JOIN core.session_drivers sd
        ON sd.session_key = p.session_key
        AND sd.driver_number = p.driver_number
    WHERE p.session_key = 9998
        AND sd.team_name ILIKE '%Ferrari%'
        AND p.pit_duration IS NOT NULL
        AND p.pit_duration > 0
        AND p.pit_duration < 60
),
field_pit AS (
    SELECT
        p.driver_number,
        p.pit_duration
    FROM raw.pit p
    WHERE p.session_key = 9998
        AND p.pit_duration IS NOT NULL
        AND p.pit_duration > 0
        AND p.pit_duration < 60
),
ferrari_stats AS (
    SELECT
        'Ferrari' AS team_label,
        ROUND(AVG(pit_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_stationary_s,
        ROUND(MIN(pit_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_stationary_s,
        ROUND(MAX(pit_duration)::numeric, 3) AS worst_stationary_s,
        COUNT(*) AS stop_count,
        STRING_AGG(full_name || ' (Lap ' || lap_number || ': ' || ROUND(pit_duration::numeric,3) || 's)', ', ' ORDER BY lap_number) AS stop_detail
    FROM ferrari_pit
),
field_stats AS (
    SELECT
        PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY pit_duration) AS field_median_s,
        ROUND(AVG(pit_duration)::numeric, 3) AS field_avg_s,
        COUNT(*) AS field_stop_count
    FROM field_pit
)
SELECT
    f.team_label,
    f.avg_stationary_s AS ferrari_avg_stationary_s,
    f.best_stationary_s AS ferrari_best_stationary_s,
    f.worst_stationary_s AS ferrari_worst_stationary_s,
    f.stop_count AS ferrari_stop_count,
    ROUND(fs.field_median_s::numeric, 3) AS field_median_stationary_s,
    fs.field_avg_s AS field_avg_stationary_s,
    fs.field_stop_count,
    ROUND((f.avg_stationary_s - fs.field_median_s)::numeric, 3) AS delta_vs_field_median_s,
    f.stop_detail
FROM ferrari_stats f
CROSS JOIN field_stats fs

results.question: Did the McLarens' decision to stay out under the lap-7 SC at Qatar 2025 cost them track position by the lap-11 restart, and by how much versus Verstappen?
results.author_note: Sky Sports / Motorsport Week: Stella admitted Qatar SC non-stop was significantly penalising; Verstappen won by 8s.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data shows that Verstappen pitted on lap 7 under the Safety Car (pit loss ~77 seconds, out on lap 8 on fresh Mediums, position P3), while both McLarens — Norris (P2 on lap 7) and Piastri — stayed out on 6-lap-old Mediums. However, the position_end_of_lap fields are largely null for laps 8–14, so we cannot confirm exact positions at the lap-11 restart from these rows. What is clear is that Verstappen emerged from his lap-7 pit stop in P3 on fresh tyres, while Norris was P2 and Piastri's position is unrecorded at that point. The data does not provide lap-11 restart positions for any of the three drivers, so a precise quantification of track position lost by the McLarens versus Verstappen by lap 11 cannot be determined from the available rows.
results.answerReasoning: The rows confirm Verstappen pitted on lap 7 (is_pit_lap=true, pit_loss_s=77.06s, out lap 8 in P3 on fresh Mediums), while Norris stayed out (P2 lap 7, 6-lap-old Mediums) and Piastri also stayed out (no position recorded). Position data after lap 7 is mostly null, preventing a definitive comparison of track positions at the lap-11 restart.
results.generationNotes: The question asks whether McLarens (driver 4 = Norris, and likely driver 81 = Piastri) staying out under the lap-7 SC at Qatar 2025 cost them track position versus Verstappen (driver 1) by the lap-11 restart. I include driver 81 (Piastri) alongside driver 4 (Norris) and driver 1 (Verstappen) since both McLarens are relevant. The query pulls: (1) lap-by-lap positions from laps 5–14 via core.laps_enriched to see position changes through the SC window and restart, including is_pit_lap/is_pit_out_lap flags and track_flag to identify SC laps; (2) stint_summary to see compound changes and who pitted; (3) analytics.pit_loss_per_circuit for anyone who did pit in that window (laps 6–11) to quantify the time cost. By comparing position_end_of_lap at lap 11 (restart) across the three drivers, and cross-referencing pit_loss_s for those who pitted vs those who stayed out, we can assess the track-position delta and whether the stay-out strategy was net positive or negative. | The error was caused by referencing 'driver_name' from core.session_drivers, which does not have that column. The correct column is 'full_name'. Fixed by: (1) aliasing full_name AS driver_name in the drivers CTE, (2) joining laps_enriched to the drivers CTE (instead of re-joining core.session_drivers directly) so d.driver_name resolves correctly, (3) also joining stint_info on lap range to provide tyre context. All other logic (lap range 5-14, pit_loss window 6-11, drivers 1/4/81 covering Verstappen and both McLarens) is preserved. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9850)
results.sql:
WITH drivers AS (
  SELECT driver_number, full_name AS driver_name, team_name
  FROM core.session_drivers
  WHERE session_key = 9850
    AND driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
),

lap_positions AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    d.driver_name,
    d.team_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_duration,
    le.position_end_of_lap,
    le.is_pit_lap,
    le.is_pit_out_lap,
    le.compound_name,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.track_flag
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  JOIN drivers d
    ON d.driver_number = le.driver_number
  WHERE le.session_key = 9850
    AND le.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
    AND le.lap_number BETWEEN 5 AND 14
),

stint_info AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    stint_number,
    compound_name,
    lap_start,
    lap_end,
    tyre_age_at_start,
    stint_length_laps
  FROM core.stint_summary
  WHERE session_key = 9850
    AND driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
    AND lap_start BETWEEN 5 AND 14
),

pit_loss AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    stop_number,
    pit_in_lap_number,
    out_lap_number,
    pit_loss_s,
    baseline_lap_s,
    new_compound_name
  FROM analytics.pit_loss_per_circuit
  WHERE session_key = 9850
    AND driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
    AND pit_in_lap_number BETWEEN 6 AND 11
)

SELECT
  lp.driver_number,
  lp.driver_name,
  lp.team_name,
  lp.lap_number,
  lp.lap_duration,
  lp.position_end_of_lap,
  lp.is_pit_lap,
  lp.is_pit_out_lap,
  lp.compound_name,
  lp.tyre_age_on_lap,
  lp.track_flag,
  si.stint_number,
  si.tyre_age_at_start,
  pl.stop_number,
  pl.pit_in_lap_number,
  pl.out_lap_number,
  pl.pit_loss_s,
  pl.baseline_lap_s,
  pl.new_compound_name AS post_pit_compound
FROM lap_positions lp
LEFT JOIN stint_info si
  ON si.driver_number = lp.driver_number
  AND lp.lap_number BETWEEN si.lap_start AND si.lap_end
LEFT JOIN pit_loss pl
  ON pl.driver_number = lp.driver_number
  AND pl.pit_in_lap_number = lp.lap_number
ORDER BY lp.lap_number, lp.position_end_of_lap
LIMIT 200

results.question: At Qatar 2025, compare the time loss for the McLarens (who stayed out under the lap-7 SC) versus Verstappen (who pitted under SC), measuring delta-to-leader at lap 11 restart and again at the chequered flag — was the swing entirely the SC pit-stop delta or did stint-management on hards add to it?
results.author_note: Mark Hughes 'Everything that fed into McLaren's Qatar debacle' — equality policy + traffic miscalc + 25-lap hard cap.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At Qatar 2025, Verstappen pitted under the lap-7 SC and incurred a ~77-second pit loss (vs. a ~85.5s baseline lap), which is an enormous cost in real time but was largely neutralised by the SC bunching the field. The McLarens (Norris and Piastri) stayed out and led at the restart, but the data does not contain explicit delta-to-leader figures at lap 11 or at the chequered flag to quantify the gap swing precisely. What the data does show is that Verstappen started P3 on the grid and finished P1 (gaining 2 positions), while Norris started P2 and finished P4 (losing 2 positions) and Piastri started P1 and finished P2 (losing 1 position). On the hard tyre in the final stint, Verstappen's degradation was near-zero (0.007 s/lap over 24 laps), while Norris degraded at 0.068 s/lap and Piastri at 0.084 s/lap over their shorter hard stints — meaning the McLarens' older/higher-degrading hard tyres in the closing laps contributed meaningfully to the position swing beyond just the SC pit-stop delta. The SC stop gave Verstappen fresh mediums for the middle phase and then fresh hards for the run to the flag, and his superior tyre management on those hards (essentially flat degradation) compounded the advantage over the McLarens, who suffered measurable hard-tyre deg in their final stints.
results.answerReasoning: FINAL_RESULT rows show Verstappen P3→P1 (+2), Norris P2→P4 (-2), Piastri P1→P2 (-1). PIT_STOPS show Verstappen's lap-7 SC stop cost ~77s pit loss. HARD_STINT_DEG shows Verstappen's hard stint (laps 34-57) had 0.007 s/lap degradation vs Norris 0.068 s/lap (laps 46-57) and Piastri 0.084 s/lap (laps 44-57). No explicit delta-to-leader values at lap 11 or flag are present in the rows, so a precise gap swing cannot be stated, but the position changes and degradation rates support the conclusion that both the SC stop strategy and hard-tyre management contributed to the outcome.
results.generationNotes:
This query builds a multi-section analysis comparing Verstappen (1), Norris (4), and Piastri (81) at Qatar 2025 (session_key=9850) across four analytical dimensions:

1. **PIT_STOPS** — Uses analytics.pit_loss_per_circuit to surface the SC pit stop cost for Verstappen (lap ~7) vs any later stops for the McLarens. pit_loss_s directly quantifies the time cost of the undercut/overcut decision.

2. **HARD_STINT_DEG** — Uses analytics.stint_degradation_curve with fuel_corrected_degradation_per_lap_s to compare how each driver's hard compound stint degraded. If McLarens stayed on older tyres post-SC, their deg rate on the long stint should be higher, quantifying the stint-management penalty beyond the pure pit-stop delta.

3. **RESTART_PACE_LAP** — Uses core.laps_enriched for laps 11-25 to show lap-by-lap pace at the restart window, revealing whether Verstappen's fresh hards gave him a pace advantage in the critical post-restart phase.

4. **POSITION_SNAPSHOT** — Uses core.race_progression_summary at laps 6, 7, 11, 12 to show positions around the SC window and restart.

5. **FINAL_RESULT** — Uses core.grid_vs_finish for the chequered flag outcome.

The UNION ALL structure returns all sections in one query. The synthesis should compare: (a) Verstappen's pit_loss_s under SC (discounted because SC bunches the field), (b) McLarens' tyre age disadvantage on hards post-lap-7, (c) deg rate differential on the long stint, and (d) whether the position swing at the flag exceeds what the SC pit delta alone would predict. | The only error was that `core.session_drivers` does not have a `driver_name` column — it uses `full_name` instead. Fixed by aliasing `full_name AS driver_name` in the `target_drivers` CTE. The `target_drivers` CTE itself was not actually joined anywhere in the original query (all other CTEs pull driver_name directly from their respective analytics/core tables which do have that column), so the fix is isolated to that CTE definition. All other table references already use the correct column names per the schema documentation. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9850)
results.sql:
WITH target_drivers AS (
  SELECT driver_number, full_name AS driver_name, team_name
  FROM core.session_drivers
  WHERE session_key = 9850
    AND driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
),

progression AS (
  SELECT
    rps.driver_number,
    rps.driver_name,
    rps.team_name,
    rps.lap_number,
    rps.position_end_of_lap
  FROM core.race_progression_summary rps
  WHERE rps.session_key = 9850
    AND rps.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
    AND rps.lap_number IN (6, 7, 11, 12)
),

pit_stops AS (
  SELECT
    plc.driver_number,
    plc.driver_name,
    plc.team_name,
    plc.stop_number,
    plc.pit_in_lap_number,
    plc.out_lap_number,
    plc.pit_loss_s,
    plc.baseline_lap_s,
    plc.new_compound_name
  FROM analytics.pit_loss_per_circuit plc
  WHERE plc.session_key = 9850
    AND plc.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
),

hard_deg AS (
  SELECT
    sdc.driver_number,
    sdc.driver_name,
    sdc.team_name,
    sdc.stint_number,
    sdc.compound_name,
    sdc.lap_start,
    sdc.lap_end,
    sdc.stint_length_laps,
    sdc.degradation_per_lap_s,
    sdc.fuel_corrected_degradation_per_lap_s,
    sdc.avg_lap_s,
    sdc.best_lap_s,
    sdc.regression_r2
  FROM analytics.stint_degradation_curve sdc
  WHERE sdc.session_key = 9850
    AND sdc.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
),

restart_pace AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.team_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_duration,
    le.compound_name,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.is_valid,
    le.position_end_of_lap
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 9850
    AND le.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
    AND le.lap_number BETWEEN 11 AND 25
),

final_positions AS (
  SELECT
    gvf.driver_number,
    gvf.driver_name,
    gvf.team_name,
    gvf.grid_position,
    gvf.finish_position,
    gvf.positions_gained
  FROM core.grid_vs_finish gvf
  WHERE gvf.session_key = 9850
    AND gvf.driver_number IN (1, 4, 81)
)

SELECT
  'PIT_STOPS' AS section,
  ps.driver_number::text,
  ps.driver_name,
  ps.team_name,
  ps.stop_number::text,
  ps.pit_in_lap_number::text AS lap_in,
  ps.out_lap_number::text AS lap_out,
  ps.new_compound_name AS compound,
  ROUND(ps.pit_loss_s::numeric, 2)::text AS pit_loss_s,
  ROUND(ps.baseline_lap_s::numeric, 2)::text AS baseline_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS lap_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_duration,
  NULL::text AS tyre_age,
  NULL::text AS position,
  NULL::text AS deg_per_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS r2
FROM pit_stops ps

UNION ALL

SELECT
  'HARD_STINT_DEG' AS section,
  hd.driver_number::text,
  hd.driver_name,
  hd.team_name,
  hd.stint_number::text,
  hd.lap_start::text AS lap_in,
  hd.lap_end::text AS lap_out,
  hd.compound_name AS compound,
  NULL::text AS pit_loss_s,
  ROUND(hd.avg_lap_s::numeric, 3)::text AS baseline_lap_s,
  hd.stint_length_laps::text AS lap_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_duration,
  NULL::text AS tyre_age,
  NULL::text AS position,
  ROUND(hd.fuel_corrected_degradation_per_lap_s::numeric, 4)::text AS deg_per_lap_s,
  ROUND(hd.regression_r2::numeric, 3)::text AS r2
FROM hard_deg hd

UNION ALL

SELECT
  'RESTART_PACE_LAP' AS section,
  rp.driver_number::text,
  rp.driver_name,
  rp.team_name,
  NULL::text AS stop_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_in,
  NULL::text AS lap_out,
  rp.compound_name AS compound,
  NULL::text AS pit_loss_s,
  NULL::text AS baseline_lap_s,
  rp.lap_number::text AS lap_number,
  ROUND(rp.lap_duration::numeric, 3)::text AS lap_duration,
  rp.tyre_age_on_lap::text AS tyre_age,
  rp.position_end_of_lap::text AS position,
  NULL::text AS deg_per_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS r2
FROM restart_pace rp
WHERE rp.is_valid = true

UNION ALL

SELECT
  'POSITION_SNAPSHOT' AS section,
  p.driver_number::text,
  p.driver_name,
  p.team_name,
  NULL::text AS stop_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_in,
  NULL::text AS lap_out,
  NULL::text AS compound,
  NULL::text AS pit_loss_s,
  NULL::text AS baseline_lap_s,
  p.lap_number::text AS lap_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_duration,
  NULL::text AS tyre_age,
  p.position_end_of_lap::text AS position,
  NULL::text AS deg_per_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS r2
FROM progression p

UNION ALL

SELECT
  'FINAL_RESULT' AS section,
  fp.driver_number::text,
  fp.driver_name,
  fp.team_name,
  NULL::text AS stop_number,
  fp.grid_position::text AS lap_in,
  fp.finish_position::text AS lap_out,
  NULL::text AS compound,
  NULL::text AS pit_loss_s,
  NULL::text AS baseline_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS lap_number,
  NULL::text AS lap_duration,
  NULL::text AS tyre_age,
  fp.finish_position::text AS position,
  fp.positions_gained::text AS deg_per_lap_s,
  NULL::text AS r2
FROM final_positions fp

ORDER BY section, driver_number, lap_in NULLS LAST

results.question: For Monaco 2025, identify drivers whose two mandatory stops were both 'free' (under VSC/SC or in a backed-up window) versus drivers who took two green-flag stops, and quantify the gap they conceded.
results.author_note: Mark Hughes 'Top teams could've gamed Monaco system too' + RaceFans Monaco rule explainer.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The available data does not include Monaco 2025 in the returned rows — the most recent sessions shown are from late 2026 and do not contain a Monaco 2025 race entry. Additionally, the query only retrieved general session metadata (dates, locations) and contains no pit-stop timing, VSC/SC flag status, or stint-level data needed to classify stops as 'free' versus green-flag or to quantify any time gap conceded. A more targeted query filtering for Monaco 2025 and joining pit-stop and flag/safety-car data would be required to answer this question.
results.answerReasoning: The returned rows are session-level records from 2026 events and do not include Monaco 2025. Even if Monaco 2025 were present, the columns (session_key, session_name, date_start, year, country_name, location) contain no pit-stop, stint, or flag-condition information necessary to distinguish free stops from green-flag stops or compute time gaps.
results.generationNotes: The query identifies all two-stop drivers in Monaco 2025 (session_key=9979) by pulling stint_summary rows where stint_number > 1 (i.e., each pit entry). For each of the first two stops it checks raw.race_control messages and core.replay_lap_frames flags within a ±2 lap window to determine whether the stop occurred under Safety Car / Virtual Safety Car conditions ('FREE') or under green-flag conditions ('GREEN'). Drivers are then classified as BOTH_FREE, BOTH_GREEN, or MIXED. Finally it joins core.driver_session_summary for race pace and finishing position, and computes each driver's average lap delta versus the BOTH_GREEN cohort's average pace — quantifying the time gap conceded (or saved) by the free-stop strategy. The result is ordered by strategy class and finish position. | The original query timed out due to correlated subqueries in pit_flags that executed per-row scans of raw.race_control and core.replay_lap_frames. The fix: (1) Pre-aggregate SC/VSC lap numbers from raw.race_control into rc_flags and from core.replay_lap_frames into frame_flags as small distinct sets — these are tiny tables scanned once. (2) Use EXISTS against those small sets instead of correlated MAX subqueries. (3) Compute the green baseline avg pace in a separate CTE (green_baseline) and CROSS JOIN it rather than using a filtered window function, which avoids re-scanning the full result set. (4) Keep two_stop_drivers filter early to minimize rows processed downstream. | auto_repair_applied | repair_failed_heuristic_used_timeout | session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate(session_key=9979)
results.sql:
SELECT session_key, session_name, date_start, year, country_name, location
    FROM core.sessions
    ORDER BY date_start DESC NULLS LAST
    LIMIT 25

results.question: On the lap-22 SC restart at Silverstone 2025, what was Verstappen's position before and after his spin, and was Piastri's brake-test penalty applied to that same restart?
results.author_note: RaceFans + F1.com British GP report: Verstappen spun P2→P10; Piastri 10s penalty for slowing too much.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data shows Verstappen was running in P2 at the start of lap 21, but ended that lap in P10 — a loss of 8 positions — which aligns with his spin occurring on lap 21 rather than the lap-22 SC restart itself. On lap 22, he remained in P10 with no positions gained or lost. Regarding Piastri's incident, the stewards placed his Safety Car infringement under investigation on lap 23, but the 10-second time penalty was only formally issued on lap 25 — so the penalty was not applied at the lap-22 restart moment, though the infringement it relates to (a Safety Car infringement) likely occurred around that restart period.
results.answerReasoning: The VER_position rows show Verstappen going from P2 to P10 on lap 21 (positions_gained = -8), indicating the spin happened on lap 21. On lap 22 he stayed at P10. Piastri's incident rows show investigation opened on lap 23 and the 10-second penalty issued on lap 25, both labeled 'Safety Car Infringement' — not a brake-test label specifically, and the penalty was not applied at the lap-22 restart itself.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks two things: (1) Verstappen's position before and after the lap-22 SC restart (including any spin effect), and (2) whether Piastri's brake-test penalty was applied around that same restart.

For (1): I pull core.race_progression_summary for driver_number=1 (Verstappen) across laps 21-24 to capture position_end_of_lap and previous_position on each lap, giving a before/after picture around lap 22. I also join core.replay_lap_frames to surface the race_control_flag (SC/VSC/green) on those laps.

For (2): I query analytics.race_control_incidents for all drivers around laps 20-25 in session 9947, specifically flagging driver_number=81 (Piastri) but also returning all incidents so the full incident picture around the restart is visible.

The two result sets are UNION ALL'd so the analyst can see both the positional data and the incident/penalty data in one result, ordered by lap number. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9947)

results.sql:
WITH restart_position AS (
    SELECT
        rps.driver_number,
        rps.driver_name,
        rps.lap_number,
        rps.position_end_of_lap,
        rps.previous_position,
        rps.positions_gained_this_lap,
        rps.opening_position
    FROM core.race_progression_summary rps
    WHERE rps.session_key = 9947
      AND rps.driver_number = 1
      AND rps.lap_number BETWEEN 21 AND 24
),
restart_frame AS (
    SELECT
        rlf.lap_number,
        rlf.race_control_flag,
        rlf.leader_driver_number,
        rlf.best_valid_lap_on_lap,
        rlf.avg_valid_lap_on_lap
    FROM core.replay_lap_frames rlf
    WHERE rlf.session_key = 9947
      AND rlf.lap_number BETWEEN 21 AND 24
),
piastri_incidents AS (
    SELECT
        rci.lap_number,
        rci.driver_number,
        rci.incident_kind,
        rci.action_status,
        rci.penalty_seconds,
        rci.message_text,
        rci.date
    FROM analytics.race_control_incidents rci
    WHERE rci.session_key = 9947
      AND rci.driver_number = 81  -- Piastri
      AND rci.lap_number BETWEEN 20 AND 25
),
all_incidents_lap22 AS (
    SELECT
        rci.lap_number,
        rci.driver_number,
        rci.incident_kind,
        rci.action_status,
        rci.penalty_seconds,
        rci.message_text,
        rci.date
    FROM analytics.race_control_incidents rci
    WHERE rci.session_key = 9947
      AND rci.lap_number BETWEEN 20 AND 25
)
SELECT
    'VER_position' AS context,
    rp.lap_number,
    rp.driver_name,
    rp.previous_position        AS position_start_of_lap,
    rp.position_end_of_lap      AS position_end_of_lap,
    rp.positions_gained_this_lap,
    rf.race_control_flag,
    NULL::integer               AS incident_driver_number,
    NULL::text                  AS incident_kind,
    NULL::text                  AS action_status,
    NULL::integer               AS penalty_seconds,
    NULL::text                  AS message_text
FROM restart_position rp
LEFT JOIN restart_frame rf ON rf.lap_number = rp.lap_number

UNION ALL

SELECT
    'INCIDENT' AS context,
    ai.lap_number,
    NULL        AS driver_name,
    NULL        AS position_start_of_lap,
    NULL        AS position_end_of_lap,
    NULL        AS positions_gained_this_lap,
    NULL        AS race_control_flag,
    ai.driver_number,
    ai.incident_kind,
    ai.action_status,
    ai.penalty_seconds,
    ai.message_text
FROM all_incidents_lap22 ai

ORDER BY lap_number, context
LIMIT 200

results.question: On the lap-1 launch at Australia 2025, did Norris or Verstappen gain more positions before the first SC, and on what compound?
results.author_note: Sky Sports + McLaren race report: Norris held off Verstappen across multiple SC bunchings.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data is insufficient to directly compare positions gained by Norris and Verstappen at the race start. Verstappen's rows show he was in P2 after lap 1 on Intermediates, but his starting position (position_before) is null, so positions gained cannot be calculated. Norris (driver #4) returned no rows at all, meaning his restart performance data is missing from the dataset. A definitive comparison cannot be made with the available data.
results.answerReasoning: All four returned rows belong to Verstappen (driver #1), with position_before null and positions_gained null, making it impossible to determine how many positions he gained. Norris (driver #4) has no rows returned. Both issues prevent a valid comparison. Verstappen was on Intermediates for lap 1, but that's the only usable fact here.
results.generationNotes: The question asks about the lap-1 race start (restart_kind = 'race_start') for Norris (driver 4) and Verstappen (driver 1) at Australia 2025 (session_key 9693). I use analytics.restart_performance filtered to race_start events for those two drivers, compute positions_gained as -position_delta (negative delta = gained positions), and join core.laps_enriched on lap_number=1 to retrieve the starting compound. Results are ordered by positions_gained descending so the bigger gainer appears first. | The only fix needed was removing the non-existent column 'normalized_compound' from the compound_info CTE. The core.laps_enriched table has 'compound_name' but not 'normalized_compound', so I simply dropped that reference and kept only 'compound_name' in the SELECT list of the CTE. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9693)
results.sql:
WITH race_start AS (
  SELECT
    rp.driver_number,
    sd.full_name AS driver_name,
    rp.restart_lap,
    rp.restart_kind,
    rp.position_before,
    rp.position_after,
    (-rp.position_delta) AS positions_gained
  FROM analytics.restart_performance rp
  JOIN core.session_drivers sd
    ON sd.session_key = rp.session_key
    AND sd.driver_number = rp.driver_number
  WHERE rp.session_key = 9693
    AND rp.restart_kind = 'race_start'
    AND rp.driver_number IN (4, 1)
),
compound_info AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    compound_name
  FROM core.laps_enriched
  WHERE session_key = 9693
    AND lap_number = 1
    AND driver_number IN (4, 1)
)
SELECT
  rs.driver_number,
  rs.driver_name,
  rs.restart_lap,
  rs.position_before,
  rs.position_after,
  rs.positions_gained,
  ci.compound_name
FROM race_start rs
LEFT JOIN compound_info ci
  ON ci.driver_number = rs.driver_number
ORDER BY rs.positions_gained DESC
LIMIT 10

results.question: Compare Russell's and Antonelli's lap-1-vs-lap-2 position gain at Canada 2025.
results.author_note: Frontstretch / news.gp Canadian GP race reports.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data is incomplete for a full comparison. Antonelli (car #12) started 4th, moved to 3rd by the end of lap 1 (gaining 1 position), but his lap-2 position is missing. Russell (car #63) started on pole (P1), but his end-of-lap-1 and end-of-lap-2 positions are both null, making any gain calculation impossible. A meaningful lap-1-vs-lap-2 comparison cannot be made with the available data.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show Antonelli's pos_after_lap1 = 3 (grid 4, so +1 gain) but pos_after_lap2 is null. Russell's pos_after_lap1 and pos_after_lap2 are both null despite a grid_position of 1. Without lap-2 data for Antonelli and any lap data for Russell, the requested comparison cannot be completed.
results.generationNotes:
We need to compare Russell (driver 12) and Antonelli (driver 63) in terms of position changes across the opening laps at Canada 2025 (session_key = 9963). The query:
1. Pulls position_end_of_lap for laps 1 and 2 from core.laps_enriched for both drivers.
2. Pivots to get pos_after_lap1 and pos_after_lap2 side by side.
3. Joins core.driver_session_summary to get grid_position for each driver.
4. Computes three deltas: grid→lap1, lap1→lap2, and grid→lap2 total. Positive values indicate forward movement (lower position number = better). | session_pin_verified(session_key=9963)
results.sql:
WITH lap_positions AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    position_end_of_lap
  FROM core.laps_enriched
  WHERE session_key = 9963
    AND driver_number IN (12, 63)
    AND lap_number IN (1, 2)
),
pivoted AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN lap_number = 1 THEN position_end_of_lap END) AS pos_after_lap1,
    MAX(CASE WHEN lap_number = 2 THEN position_end_of_lap END) AS pos_after_lap2
  FROM lap_positions
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name
),
grid AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    grid_position
  FROM core.driver_session_summary
  WHERE session_key = 9963
    AND driver_number IN (12, 63)
)
SELECT
  p.driver_number,
  p.driver_name,
  g.grid_position,
  p.pos_after_lap1,
  p.pos_after_lap2,
  -- Gain from grid to end of lap 1 (positive = moved forward)
  g.grid_position - p.pos_after_lap1 AS gain_grid_to_lap1,
  -- Gain from lap 1 to end of lap 2 (positive = moved forward)
  p.pos_after_lap1 - p.pos_after_lap2 AS gain_lap1_to_lap2,
  -- Total gain from grid to end of lap 2
  g.grid_position - p.pos_after_lap2 AS total_gain_through_lap2
FROM pivoted p
JOIN grid g USING (driver_number)
ORDER BY p.driver_number

results.question: Across the three SC restarts of the 2025 Australian GP, quantify field-bunching at green-flag and 1 lap later — did the bunched restart erase Norris's lead, or did Verstappen lose ground in sector 2?
results.author_note: F1.com Australian GP report + Motor Authority on third SC bunching; Norris won by 0.895s.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The query returned insufficient data to answer the question. Only 2 rows came back, both for restart lap 51, and all key fields — driver positions, top-5 bunching details, Norris's and Verstappen's positions, and restart performance metrics — are null. There is no evidence in the returned rows of three SC restarts, no field-bunching gap data between P1 and P5, and no position information for either Norris or Verstappen at any restart. It is therefore not possible to determine whether the bunched restart erased Norris's lead or whether Verstappen lost ground in sector 2.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show only restart_lap 51 with drivers_in_frame of 2 and 4 respectively, but top5_field_bunching and all driver-specific columns are null. The analytics.restart_performance and core.race_progression_summary tables either lack the required data for session 9693 or the joins failed to match records, leaving no usable values to support any claim about field bunching, Norris's lead, or Verstappen's sector 2 performance.
results.generationNotes: This query addresses the 2025 Australian GP (session_key=9693) SC restart analysis across three safety car restarts. It: (1) identifies all SC restart laps from analytics.restart_performance filtered to sc_restart kind; (2) pulls field positions at the restart lap and 1 lap later from core.race_progression_summary to measure bunching (top-5 positions as a proxy for field compression); (3) focuses on Norris (driver 4) and Verstappen (driver 1) specifically; (4) joins analytics.restart_performance for position_before/after and positions_gained (-position_delta) for those two drivers at each SC restart. This allows comparison of whether Norris's lead was erased or Verstappen lost ground in the restart dynamics. Note: sector-2 specific data would require telemetry/sector splits which are not available in restart_performance — the position delta is the best available proxy for restart performance. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9693)
results.sql:
WITH sc_restarts AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT restart_lap
    FROM analytics.restart_performance
    WHERE session_key = 9693
      AND restart_kind = 'sc_restart'
    ORDER BY restart_lap
),
restart_positions AS (
    -- Position at restart lap and 1 lap later for all drivers
    SELECT
        rp.lap_number,
        rp.driver_number,
        rp.driver_name,
        rp.team_name,
        rp.position_end_of_lap,
        sr.restart_lap,
        CASE WHEN rp.lap_number = sr.restart_lap THEN 'restart_lap'
             WHEN rp.lap_number = sr.restart_lap + 1 THEN 'lap_after'
        END AS lap_phase
    FROM core.race_progression_summary rp
    JOIN sc_restarts sr
      ON rp.lap_number IN (sr.restart_lap, sr.restart_lap + 1)
    WHERE rp.session_key = 9693
),
bunching AS (
    -- Field bunching: gap between P1 and P5 at restart vs lap after
    SELECT
        restart_lap,
        lap_phase,
        COUNT(DISTINCT driver_number) AS drivers_in_frame,
        MIN(position_end_of_lap) AS p1_pos,
        MAX(CASE WHEN position_end_of_lap <= 5 THEN position_end_of_lap END) AS p5_pos,
        -- Collect top-5 drivers to see bunching
        STRING_AGG(
            CASE WHEN position_end_of_lap <= 5
                 THEN position_end_of_lap::text || ':' || driver_name
            END,
            ', ' ORDER BY position_end_of_lap
        ) AS top5_positions
    FROM restart_positions
    GROUP BY restart_lap, lap_phase
),
norris_verstappen AS (
    -- Focus on Norris (4) and Verstappen (1) across SC restarts
    SELECT
        rp.restart_lap,
        rp.lap_phase,
        rp.driver_number,
        rp.driver_name,
        rp.position_end_of_lap
    FROM restart_positions rp
    WHERE rp.driver_number IN (1, 4)
),
restart_perf AS (
    SELECT
        arp.restart_lap,
        arp.driver_number,
        arp.driver_name,
        arp.team_name,
        arp.position_before,
        arp.position_after,
        -arp.position_delta AS positions_gained
    FROM analytics.restart_performance arp
    WHERE arp.session_key = 9693
      AND arp.restart_kind = 'sc_restart'
      AND arp.driver_number IN (1, 4)
)
SELECT
    b.restart_lap,
    b.lap_phase,
    b.drivers_in_frame,
    b.top5_positions AS top5_field_bunching,
    nv.driver_number,
    nv.driver_name,
    nv.position_end_of_lap AS position,
    rp.position_before,
    rp.position_after,
    rp.positions_gained
FROM bunching b
LEFT JOIN norris_verstappen nv
    ON b.restart_lap = nv.restart_lap
   AND b.lap_phase = nv.lap_phase
LEFT JOIN restart_perf rp
    ON b.restart_lap = rp.restart_lap
   AND nv.driver_number = rp.driver_number
ORDER BY b.restart_lap, b.lap_phase DESC, nv.driver_number

results.question: What was the fresh-vs-used delta on the medium compound for Norris when McLaren switched him to a one-stop in Hungary 2025?
results.author_note: The Race 'McLaren didn't intend strategy switch' on Norris's stretched medium one-stop.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data only contains one medium compound stint for Norris in Hungary 2025 (session 9928), and it is classified as 'used' (stint 1, laps 1–31, tyre age at start = 0). There is no separate 'fresh' medium stint recorded, so a fresh-vs-used delta cannot be calculated — both delta values return null. With only a single medium stint available, the premise of comparing fresh versus used mediums for a one-stop strategy switch cannot be supported by the available data.
results.answerReasoning: The query returned only one row (tyre_state = 'used'), meaning there is no 'fresh' medium stint to compare against. The delta columns (used_vs_fresh_avg_delta_s and used_vs_fresh_best_delta_s) are both null because the CASE expressions for 'fresh' return no values. Interestingly, tyre_age_at_start = 0 for the 'used' stint suggests a possible data classification issue, but regardless, no fresh/used comparison is possible with a single stint.
results.generationNotes: The question asks for the fresh vs used tyre delta on the Medium compound for Norris (driver 4) in Hungary 2025 (session_key 9928). I use core.stint_summary which has compound_name, fresh_tyre, tyre_age_at_start, avg_valid_lap, best_valid_lap, and degradation_per_lap at the stint grain. I filter to session_key=9928, driver_number=4, and MEDIUM compound. I then group by fresh/used (via the fresh_tyre boolean) and compute average lap time, best lap, and degradation per lap for each group. A window function computes the used-minus-fresh delta for both avg and best lap times, giving a direct comparison of pace penalty from running used mediums vs fresh mediums — which is the core of the one-stop strategy question. | The original query incorrectly used window function syntax (OVER ()) inside a GROUP BY query, which is invalid in PostgreSQL when mixed with aggregation in that way. The fix moves the cross-stint delta calculation into a separate CTE ('deltas') that aggregates fresh vs used avg and best lap times using conditional MAX, then CROSS JOINs it back to the per-tyre-state 'agg' CTE. This cleanly separates the per-group aggregation from the cross-group delta computation, eliminating the syntax error. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH norris_medium_stints AS (
    SELECT
        ss.stint_number,
        ss.compound_name,
        ss.tyre_age_at_start,
        ss.fresh_tyre,
        ss.lap_start,
        ss.lap_end,
        ss.stint_length_laps,
        ss.avg_valid_lap,
        ss.best_valid_lap,
        ss.degradation_per_lap
    FROM core.stint_summary ss
    WHERE ss.session_key = 9928
      AND ss.driver_number = 4
      AND ss.compound_name ILIKE '%MEDIUM%'
),
fresh_used AS (
    SELECT
        stint_number,
        compound_name,
        tyre_age_at_start,
        fresh_tyre,
        lap_start,
        lap_end,
        stint_length_laps,
        avg_valid_lap,
        best_valid_lap,
        degradation_per_lap,
        CASE WHEN fresh_tyre THEN 'fresh' ELSE 'used' END AS tyre_state
    FROM norris_medium_stints
),
agg AS (
    SELECT
        tyre_state,
        COUNT(*) AS stint_count,
        MIN(tyre_age_at_start) AS min_tyre_age_at_start,
        MAX(tyre_age_at_start) AS max_tyre_age_at_start,
        AVG(avg_valid_lap) AS avg_lap_s,
        MIN(best_valid_lap) AS best_lap_s,
        AVG(degradation_per_lap) AS avg_deg_per_lap_s,
        STRING_AGG(stint_number::text, ', ' ORDER BY stint_number) AS stint_numbers,
        STRING_AGG(lap_start::text || '-' || lap_end::text, ', ' ORDER BY stint_number) AS lap_ranges
    FROM fresh_used
    GROUP BY tyre_state
),
deltas AS (
    SELECT
        MAX(CASE WHEN tyre_state = 'used' THEN avg_lap_s END) -
        MAX(CASE WHEN tyre_state = 'fresh' THEN avg_lap_s END) AS used_vs_fresh_avg_delta_s,
        MAX(CASE WHEN tyre_state = 'used' THEN best_lap_s END) -
        MAX(CASE WHEN tyre_state = 'fresh' THEN best_lap_s END) AS used_vs_fresh_best_delta_s
    FROM agg
)
SELECT
    a.tyre_state,
    a.stint_count,
    a.min_tyre_age_at_start,
    a.max_tyre_age_at_start,
    ROUND(a.avg_lap_s::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap_s,
    ROUND(a.best_lap_s::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_s,
    ROUND(a.avg_deg_per_lap_s::numeric, 4) AS avg_deg_per_lap_s,
    a.stint_numbers,
    a.lap_ranges,
    ROUND(d.used_vs_fresh_avg_delta_s::numeric, 3) AS used_vs_fresh_avg_delta_s,
    ROUND(d.used_vs_fresh_best_delta_s::numeric, 3) AS used_vs_fresh_best_delta_s
FROM agg a
CROSS JOIN deltas d
ORDER BY a.tyre_state DESC

results.question: How did the closing-stint hard-tyre pace differ between the one-stoppers (Norris, Leclerc) and two-stoppers (Piastri) in the final 15 laps at Hungary 2025?
results.author_note: Motorsport Week + RaceFans Hungary recap: Piastri's tyre offset wasn't enough to clear Norris despite late-race charge.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Lando NORRIS: one-stop strategy (1 stop); Charles LECLERC: two-stop strategy (2 stops); Oscar PIASTRI: two-stop strategy (2 stops).
results.answerReasoning: The rows directly provide avg_hard_pace_s, best_hard_lap_s, stddev_hard_pace_s, tyre age windows, and pit_stop_count for all three drivers. Piastri (2-stop, youngest tyres) was fastest, Norris (1-stop, oldest tyres) was second but remarkably consistent, and Leclerc (2-stop, intermediate tyre age) was slowest and most variable. The degradation_per_lap values are negative (improving pace or noise), so the tyre age and pace comparison is the key differentiator. | answer_guard:strategy_stop_count_consistency | stop_count_consistent_with_stints
results.generationNotes:
The question asks to compare closing-stint hard-tyre pace in the final 15 laps between one-stoppers (Norris=4, Leclerc=16) and two-stopper (Piastri=81) at Hungary 2025 (session_key=9928). I:
1. Identify the three drivers by number (4=Norris, 16=Leclerc, 81=Piastri) from core.session_drivers.
2. Pull their strategy type and pit stop count from core.strategy_summary to confirm 1-stop vs 2-stop classification.
3. Find their closing HARD-compound stint from core.stint_summary, filtering for stints ending within the final 15 laps of the race.
4. Aggregate valid HARD-compound laps from core.laps_enriched in the final 15 laps window (lap_number >= max_lap - 14) to get avg, best, worst, stddev pace and tyre age range.
5. Join everything together to produce a side-by-side comparison showing strategy type, closing stint details, and pace metrics for the final 15 laps. | The original query timed out because the subqueries `SELECT MAX(lap_number) FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = 9928` were executed multiple times inline. The fix materialises `max_lap` once as a CTE and uses CROSS JOIN to reference it in both `final_laps_agg` and `closing_stint`, eliminating repeated full-table scans. The `closing_stint` CTE uses DISTINCT ON to pick the last hard-tyre stint per driver efficiently. The main SELECT is simplified to join directly from `core.session_drivers` rather than a redundant `target_drivers` CTE, keeping the query lean and within the timeout budget. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH max_lap AS (
  SELECT MAX(lap_number) AS ml
  FROM core.laps_enriched
  WHERE session_key = 9928
),
final_laps_agg AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    COUNT(*) AS valid_hard_laps_in_final_15,
    ROUND(AVG(le.lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_hard_pace_s,
    ROUND(MIN(le.lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_hard_lap_s,
    ROUND(MAX(le.lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS worst_hard_lap_s,
    ROUND(STDDEV(le.lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS stddev_hard_pace_s,
    MIN(le.tyre_age_on_lap) AS min_tyre_age,
    MAX(le.tyre_age_on_lap) AS max_tyre_age
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  CROSS JOIN max_lap mx
  WHERE le.session_key = 9928
    AND le.driver_number IN (4, 16, 81)
    AND le.compound_name ILIKE '%HARD%'
    AND le.is_valid = TRUE
    AND le.lap_number >= mx.ml - 14
  GROUP BY le.driver_number
),
closing_stint AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT ON (st.driver_number)
    st.driver_number,
    st.stint_number,
    st.compound_name,
    st.lap_start,
    st.lap_end,
    st.stint_length_laps,
    st.avg_valid_lap,
    st.best_valid_lap,
    st.degradation_per_lap
  FROM core.stint_summary st
  CROSS JOIN max_lap mx
  WHERE st.session_key = 9928
    AND st.driver_number IN (4, 16, 81)
    AND st.compound_name ILIKE '%HARD%'
    AND st.lap_end >= mx.ml - 14
  ORDER BY st.driver_number, st.stint_number DESC
)
SELECT
  sd.driver_number,
  sd.full_name,
  sd.team_name,
  str.pit_stop_count,
  str.strategy_type,
  cs.compound_name AS closing_compound,
  cs.lap_start AS closing_stint_lap_start,
  cs.lap_end AS closing_stint_lap_end,
  cs.stint_length_laps AS closing_stint_total_laps,
  fla.valid_hard_laps_in_final_15,
  fla.avg_hard_pace_s,
  fla.best_hard_lap_s,
  fla.worst_hard_lap_s,
  fla.stddev_hard_pace_s,
  fla.min_tyre_age AS tyre_age_start_of_window,
  fla.max_tyre_age AS tyre_age_end_of_window,
  cs.degradation_per_lap AS closing_stint_deg_per_lap_s
FROM core.session_drivers sd
LEFT JOIN core.strategy_summary str
  ON str.session_key = 9928 AND str.driver_number = sd.driver_number
LEFT JOIN closing_stint cs
  ON cs.driver_number = sd.driver_number
LEFT JOIN final_laps_agg fla
  ON fla.driver_number = sd.driver_number
WHERE sd.session_key = 9928
  AND sd.driver_number IN (4, 16, 81)
ORDER BY str.pit_stop_count ASC, sd.driver_number ASC
LIMIT 20

results.question: What was Ferrari's average speed-trap reading across the Las Vegas 2025 weekend?
results.author_note: PlanetF1 Vegas straight-line recap pieces averaging speed-trap across sessions.

results.answer: Across the Las Vegas 2025 weekend, Ferrari's two drivers averaged the following speed-trap (ST) readings by session: Practice 1 — Leclerc 353 kph, Hamilton 351 kph; Practice 2 — Leclerc 349 kph, Hamilton 359 kph; Practice 3 — both at 354 kph; Qualifying — Leclerc 328 kph, Hamilton 333 kph; Race — Leclerc 355 kph, Hamilton 364 kph. Averaging all 10 session-driver readings, Ferrari's overall mean speed-trap reading across the weekend was approximately 350 kph, with the Race being their fastest session (up to 364 kph for Hamilton) and Qualifying the slowest.

Summing all avg_st_speed_kph values: 353+351+349+359+354+354+328+333+355+364 = 3500, divided by 10 = 350.0 kph overall average. The Race session showed the highest trap speeds while Qualifying showed the lowest, likely due to fewer high-speed laps completed in qualifying.

results.sql:
SELECT
  sld.driver_number,
  sld.driver_name,
  sld.team_name,
  s.session_name,
  s.session_key,
  ROUND(AVG(sld.st_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND(AVG(sld.i2_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_i2_speed_kph,
  ROUND(AVG(sld.i1_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_i1_speed_kph,
  ROUND(AVG(sld.avg_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_kph
FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
JOIN core.sessions s ON s.session_key = sld.session_key
WHERE s.meeting_key = 1274
  AND LOWER(sld.team_name) LIKE '%ferrari%'
GROUP BY
  sld.driver_number,
  sld.driver_name,
  sld.team_name,
  s.session_name,
  s.session_key
ORDER BY s.session_key, sld.driver_name

results.question: Did Mercedes's straight-line speed advantage at Baku 2025 hold up between qualifying and the race?
results.author_note: Motorsport.com qualifying-trim vs race-trim speed-trap comparisons after Baku.

results.answer: Mercedes did not have a clear straight-line speed advantage in qualifying — both Russell (341 km/h) and Antonelli (340 km/h) were essentially at the field average of 340.4 km/h, and 7–8 km/h below the fastest car (348 km/h). In the race, however, Mercedes improved noticeably: Russell hit 350 km/h and Antonelli 348 km/h, both above the field average of 345.8 km/h by +4.2 and +2.2 km/h respectively. That said, the field maximum in the race jumped to 361 km/h, leaving Mercedes still 11–13 km/h short of the outright fastest car. So Mercedes went from slightly below average in qualifying to modestly above average in the race, but never held a dominant straight-line speed advantage at Baku 2025. Caveats: core.laps_enriched has no rows for session 9900. core.driver_session_summary has no rows for session 9900.
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide speed trap (st_speed_kph) values for both Mercedes drivers in qualifying and the race, along with field averages and maximums. In qualifying, both drivers were within 1 km/h of the field average and well below the max. In the race, both were above the field average but still far from the field maximum, indicating a relative improvement but no dominant advantage.

results.sql:
WITH baku_2025_sessions AS (
  SELECT s.session_key, s.session_type, s.session_name
  FROM core.sessions s
  WHERE s.meeting_key = 1269
    AND s.session_type IN ('Qualifying', 'Race', 'Sprint', 'Sprint Qualifying')
),
merc_drivers AS (
  SELECT sd.session_key, sd.driver_number, sd.full_name, sd.team_name
  FROM core.session_drivers sd
  WHERE sd.meeting_key = 1269
    AND sd.team_name ILIKE '%mercedes%'
),
speed_data AS (
  SELECT
    sld.session_key,
    bs.session_type,
    bs.session_name,
    sld.driver_number,
    md.full_name,
    md.team_name,
    sld.st_speed_kph,
    sld.i2_speed_kph,
    sld.i1_speed_kph,
    sld.avg_speed_kph
  FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
  JOIN baku_2025_sessions bs ON bs.session_key = sld.session_key
  JOIN merc_drivers md ON md.session_key = sld.session_key AND md.driver_number = sld.driver_number
),
field_avg AS (
  SELECT
    sld.session_key,
    AVG(sld.st_speed_kph)  AS field_avg_st_speed,
    AVG(sld.i2_speed_kph)  AS field_avg_i2_speed,
    AVG(sld.avg_speed_kph) AS field_avg_avg_speed,
    MAX(sld.st_speed_kph)  AS field_max_st_speed
  FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
  JOIN baku_2025_sessions bs ON bs.session_key = sld.session_key
  GROUP BY sld.session_key
)
SELECT
  sp.session_name,
  sp.session_type,
  sp.driver_number,
  sp.full_name,
  sp.team_name,
  ROUND(sp.st_speed_kph::numeric, 2)          AS merc_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND(sp.i2_speed_kph::numeric, 2)          AS merc_i2_speed_kph,
  ROUND(sp.i1_speed_kph::numeric, 2)          AS merc_i1_speed_kph,
  ROUND(sp.avg_speed_kph::numeric, 2)         AS merc_avg_speed_kph,
  ROUND(fa.field_avg_st_speed::numeric, 2)    AS field_avg_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND(fa.field_max_st_speed::numeric, 2)    AS field_max_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND((sp.st_speed_kph - fa.field_avg_st_speed)::numeric, 2) AS delta_vs_field_avg_kph,
  ROUND((sp.st_speed_kph - fa.field_max_st_speed)::numeric, 2) AS delta_vs_field_max_kph
FROM speed_data sp
JOIN field_avg fa ON fa.session_key = sp.session_key
ORDER BY sp.session_type, sp.driver_number

Verstappen's higher avg_speed_kph indicates better overall lap pace despite lower straight-line speeds.

results.sql:
SELECT
  sld.driver_number,
  sld.driver_name,
  sld.team_name,
  ROUND(sld.i1_speed_kph::numeric, 2)  AS i1_speed_kph,
  ROUND(sld.i2_speed_kph::numeric, 2)  AS i2_speed_kph,
  ROUND(sld.st_speed_kph::numeric, 2)  AS st_speed_trap_kph,
  ROUND(sld.avg_speed_kph::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_kph,
  -- deltas relative to Verstappen (driver 1)
  ROUND((sld.i1_speed_kph - ref.i1_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS delta_i1_vs_ver,
  ROUND((sld.i2_speed_kph - ref.i2_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS delta_i2_vs_ver,
  ROUND((sld.st_speed_kph - ref.st_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS delta_st_vs_ver
FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
CROSS JOIN (

results.question: Across i1, i2, and speed-trap at Las Vegas 2025, where did Mercedes's straight-line advantage over Red Bull come from — early acceleration (i1), mid-straight (i2), or terminal (trap)? And did it hold DRS-free in the race?
results.author_note: The Race / Mark Hughes Vegas long-form analyses decomposing straight-line speed into acceleration phases.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Mercedes's straight-line advantage over Red Bull at Las Vegas 2025 grew progressively along the straight and peaked at the speed trap (terminal speed). At i1 Mercedes led by +3.0 km/h (326 vs 323), the gap widened to +4.0 km/h at i2 (336 vs 332), and reached its maximum of +6.0 km/h at the trap (355.5 vs 349.5). However, this top-speed edge did not translate into an overall lap-speed advantage: Red Bull's average speed across the lap was marginally higher (213.24 vs 212.95 km/h, a gap of just 0.29 km/h), suggesting Mercedes's straight-line gains were offset by Red Bull's pace elsewhere on the circuit. The data does not include DRS-status flags, so a clean DRS-free comparison cannot be confirmed from these rows alone.
results.answerReasoning: The delta values show a clear progression: delta_i1 = +3.0, delta_i2 = +4.0, delta_st = +6.0, confirming the advantage built along the straight and peaked at the trap. The query's own CASE logic labels this 'Terminal speed (trap)'. The avg_speed_kph values (Mercedes 212.95, Red Bull 213.24) show Red Bull was marginally faster overall, indicating the straight-line edge didn't dominate the full lap. No DRS-on/off field is present in the rows.
results.generationNotes: The query uses analytics.straight_line_dominance (session_key=9858, Las Vegas 2025 race) to pull i1_speed_kph (90th pctile proxy for early acceleration), i2_speed_kph (95th pctile proxy for mid-straight), and st_speed_kph (max speed proxy for terminal/trap) for all Mercedes and Red Bull drivers in that session. It averages each metric per team, computes Mercedes-minus-Red Bull deltas at each speed point, and adds a plain-language label identifying which zone shows the largest Mercedes advantage. A per-driver JSON detail array is included for transparency. Note: these are statistical proxies from aggregated telemetry samples — they are not spatially attributed to specific DRS zones, so the 'DRS-free' dimension cannot be directly isolated from this matview alone; the synthesis should caveat that the race session naturally includes both DRS-active and DRS-inactive straights mixed together. | The only fix needed was replacing sd.driver_name with sd.full_name AS driver_name in both the mercedes_drivers and redbull_drivers CTEs, since core.session_drivers uses full_name rather than driver_name. All other logic remains identical. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9858)

previewRows.advantage_zone: Terminal speed (trap) — Mercedes advantage peaks at speed trap

results.sql:
WITH mercedes_drivers AS (
    SELECT sd.driver_number, sd.full_name AS driver_name, sd.team_name
    FROM core.session_drivers sd
    WHERE sd.session_key = 9858
      AND sd.team_name ILIKE '%Mercedes%'
),
redbull_drivers AS (
    SELECT sd.driver_number, sd.full_name AS driver_name, sd.team_name
    FROM core.session_drivers sd
    WHERE sd.session_key = 9858
      AND sd.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%'
),
all_drivers AS (
    SELECT driver_number, driver_name, team_name FROM mercedes_drivers
    UNION ALL
    SELECT driver_number, driver_name, team_name FROM redbull_drivers
),
speed_stats AS (
    SELECT
        sld.driver_number,
        sld.driver_name,
        sld.team_name,
        sld.i1_speed_kph,
        sld.i2_speed_kph,
        sld.st_speed_kph,
        sld.avg_speed_kph
    FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
    WHERE sld.session_key = 9858
      AND sld.driver_number IN (SELECT driver_number FROM all_drivers)
),
team_avg AS (
    SELECT
        ad.team_name,
        ROUND(AVG(ss.i1_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS avg_i1_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(ss.i2_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS avg_i2_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(ss.st_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS avg_st_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(ss.avg_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_overall_kph,
        COUNT(*)                                  AS driver_count
    FROM speed_stats ss
    JOIN all_drivers ad USING (driver_number)
    GROUP BY ad.team_name
),
delta AS (
    SELECT
        m.team_name                                                   AS team_mercedes,
        r.team_name                                                   AS team_redbull,
        m.avg_i1_kph                                                  AS merc_i1,
        r.avg_i1_kph                                                  AS rb_i1,
        ROUND((m.avg_i1_kph - r.avg_i1_kph)::numeric, 2)            AS delta_i1_kph,
        m.avg_i2_kph                                                  AS merc_i2,
        r.avg_i2_kph                                                  AS rb_i2,
        ROUND((m.avg_i2_kph - r.avg_i2_kph)::numeric, 2)            AS delta_i2_kph,
        m.avg_st_kph                                                  AS merc_st,
        r.avg_st_kph                                                  AS rb_st,
        ROUND((m.avg_st_kph - r.avg_st_kph)::numeric, 2)            AS delta_st_kph,
        m.avg_overall_kph                                             AS merc_avg,
        r.avg_overall_kph                                             AS rb_avg,
        ROUND((m.avg_overall_kph - r.avg_overall_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS delta_avg_kph
    FROM team_avg m
    CROSS JOIN team_avg r
    WHERE m.team_name ILIKE '%Mercedes%'
      AND r.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%'
)
SELECT
    d.*,
    CASE
        WHEN d.delta_i1_kph >= d.delta_i2_kph AND d.delta_i1_kph >= d.delta_st_kph
            THEN 'Early acceleration (i1) — Mercedes advantage peaks at launch'
        WHEN d.delta_i2_kph >= d.delta_i1_kph AND d.delta_i2_kph >= d.delta_st_kph
            THEN 'Mid-straight (i2) — Mercedes advantage peaks mid-straight'
        ELSE 'Terminal speed (trap) — Mercedes advantage peaks at speed trap'
    END AS advantage_zone,
    (
        SELECT json_agg(row_to_json(ss) ORDER BY ss.team_name, ss.driver_number)
        FROM speed_stats ss
    ) AS per_driver_detail
FROM delta d

results.question: Compare Ferrari's qualifying-trim vs race-trim straight-line speed at Monza 2025 across all three intermediates — did the trim change cost more on i1 or i2?
results.author_note: Motorsport.com Italian GP setup-trim analyses on Ferrari's low-downforce package.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: For Charles Leclerc at Monza 2025, Ferrari's race trim actually showed higher speeds than qualifying trim across all three speed traps — not lower — suggesting the data may reflect different running conditions rather than a pure trim comparison. At the speed trap (ST), race trim was 3 km/h faster (355 vs 352 km/h). At i1, race trim was 11 km/h faster (323 vs 312 km/h), and at i2, race trim was 2 km/h faster (330 vs 328 km/h). The largest delta between the two trims appeared at i1 (11 km/h gap), making i1 the intermediate where the trim difference was most pronounced. Only Leclerc (car #16) returned data; no results were available for car #18 (Oliver Bearman) to complete the Ferrari team comparison.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show positive deltas (race minus qualifying) at all three intermediates for Leclerc, meaning race trim was faster — possibly due to slipstreaming or different fuel/tyre conditions. The trim_cost_verdict field labels i1 as having the larger absolute delta (11 km/h vs 2 km/h at i2), which directly answers which intermediate showed the bigger difference. Only one driver row was returned, so the comparison is incomplete for the full Ferrari lineup.
results.generationNotes: The question asks to compare Ferrari's qualifying vs race straight-line speed at Monza 2025 across the three speed trap intermediates (ST, I1, I2) for drivers 16 and 18. The analytics.straight_line_dominance table holds st_speed_kph, i1_speed_kph, i2_speed_kph, and avg_speed_kph per session per driver. I join it against core.sessions filtered to meeting_key=1268 for Qualifying and Race session types, then pivot to get qualifying and race values side by side. The delta columns (race minus qualifying) show how much speed was lost/gained in race trim at each intermediate, and a verdict column flags whether i1 or i2 saw the larger trim cost. | The only fix needed was replacing sd.driver_name with sd.full_name, since core.session_drivers uses full_name instead of driver_name. All other logic remains identical — joining ferrari sessions (Qualifying + Race) for meeting_key 1268 against analytics.straight_line_dominance for Ferrari drivers (16, 18), pivoting by session_type, computing deltas, and determining whether i1 or i2 saw the larger trim-change speed cost. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate(session_key=9908)

previewRows.trim_cost_verdict: i1 lost more speed in race trim

results.sql:
WITH ferrari_sessions AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT s.session_key, s.session_type, s.session_name
  FROM core.sessions s
  WHERE s.meeting_key = 1268
    AND s.session_type IN ('Qualifying', 'Race')
),
ferrari_speed AS (
  SELECT
    fs.session_type,
    fs.session_name,
    sd.driver_number,
    sd.full_name AS driver_name,
    sd.team_name,
    sld.st_speed_kph,
    sld.i1_speed_kph,
    sld.i2_speed_kph,
    sld.avg_speed_kph
  FROM ferrari_sessions fs
  JOIN analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
    ON sld.session_key = fs.session_key
  JOIN core.session_drivers sd
    ON sd.session_key = fs.session_key
    AND sd.driver_number = sld.driver_number
  WHERE sd.team_name ILIKE '%ferrari%'
    AND sld.driver_number IN (16, 18)
),
pivoted AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Qualifying' THEN st_speed_kph END) AS quali_st_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Race'       THEN st_speed_kph END) AS race_st_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Qualifying' THEN i1_speed_kph END) AS quali_i1_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Race'       THEN i1_speed_kph END) AS race_i1_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Qualifying' THEN i2_speed_kph END) AS quali_i2_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Race'       THEN i2_speed_kph END) AS race_i2_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Qualifying' THEN avg_speed_kph END) AS quali_avg_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN session_type = 'Race'       THEN avg_speed_kph END) AS race_avg_speed
  FROM ferrari_speed
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name
)
SELECT
  driver_number,
  driver_name,
  ROUND(quali_st_speed::numeric, 2)  AS quali_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND(race_st_speed::numeric, 2)   AS race_st_speed_kph,
  ROUND((race_st_speed - quali_st_speed)::numeric, 2) AS st_delta_kph,
  ROUND(quali_i1_speed::numeric, 2)  AS quali_i1_speed_kph,
  ROUND(race_i1_speed::numeric, 2)   AS race_i1_speed_kph,
  ROUND((race_i1_speed - quali_i1_speed)::numeric, 2) AS i1_delta_kph,
  ROUND(quali_i2_speed::numeric, 2)  AS quali_i2_speed_kph,
  ROUND(race_i2_speed::numeric, 2)   AS race_i2_speed_kph,
  ROUND((race_i2_speed - quali_i2_speed)::numeric, 2) AS i2_delta_kph,
  ROUND(quali_avg_speed::numeric, 2) AS quali_avg_speed_kph,
  ROUND(race_avg_speed::numeric, 2)  AS race_avg_speed_kph,
  ROUND((race_avg_speed - quali_avg_speed)::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_delta_kph,
  CASE
    WHEN ABS(race_i1_speed - quali_i1_speed) > ABS(race_i2_speed - quali_i2_speed)
    THEN 'i1 lost more speed in race trim'
    WHEN ABS(race_i2_speed - quali_i2_speed) > ABS(race_i1_speed - quali_i1_speed)
    THEN 'i2 lost more speed in race trim'
    ELSE 'equal delta on i1 and i2'
  END AS trim_cost_verdict
FROM pivoted
ORDER BY driver_number

results.question: Across Baku, Monza, and Spa 2025, was Red Bull's straight-line deficit to McLaren consistent, or venue-specific to low-downforce circuits?
results.author_note: Edd Straw / The Race season-arc analyses of Red Bull's power-vs-drag tradeoffs across European low-downforce triple.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Red Bull's straight-line deficit to McLaren was not consistent — it was highly venue-specific rather than a uniform low-downforce weakness. At Baku, Red Bull was actually faster than McLaren across most speed traps (e.g., +28.5 km/h at I1, +21.5 km/h at I2), suggesting a higher-downforce McLaren setup there. At Monza, the picture was mixed: Red Bull had a +7 km/h speed trap advantage but trailed McLaren by 4.18 km/h on average speed. At Spa, the gap was negligible (Red Bull within 1 km/h of McLaren on average, with a slight +4.5 km/h speed trap edge). There is no consistent pattern of Red Bull being slower in a straight line at these three low-downforce venues — the deltas vary significantly by circuit and measurement point.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show Baku with Red Bull ahead on I1 (+28.5) and I2 (+21.5) but only marginally on the speed trap (+2.0), while overall avg speed shows Red Bull much higher (+27.33), likely reflecting different circuit layouts or data anomalies. At Monza, Red Bull trails on avg speed (-4.18) but leads on speed trap (+7.0). At Spa, differences are tiny (avg delta -0.88, ST delta +4.5). This inconsistency across venues means no single narrative of 'Red Bull deficit on low-downforce circuits' holds up.
results.generationNotes: The question asks whether Red Bull's straight-line speed deficit to McLaren is consistent or venue-specific across Baku, Monza, and Spa in 2025. The best contract for this is analytics.straight_line_dominance, which provides avg_speed_kph, st_speed_kph (speed trap), i1_speed_kph, and i2_speed_kph per driver per session. I filter to 2025 Race sessions at those three venues, then aggregate by team (Red Bull vs McLaren) per circuit, and pivot to compute deltas. This reveals whether the gap is uniform (consistent) or varies by venue (venue-specific). The runtime context provides session_key 9904 but the question spans three venues, so I use the sessions table to resolve all three 2025 race sessions dynamically. | session_pin_unverifiable_no_literal_session_key_predicate(session_key=9904)
results.sql:
WITH target_sessions AS (
    SELECT
        s.session_key,
        s.meeting_key,
        s.circuit_short_name,
        s.country_name,
        s.year
    FROM core.sessions s
    WHERE s.year = 2025
      AND s.session_type = 'Race'
      AND (
          s.circuit_short_name ILIKE '%Baku%'
          OR s.circuit_short_name ILIKE '%Monza%'
          OR s.circuit_short_name ILIKE '%Spa%'
          OR s.country_name ILIKE '%Azerbaijan%'
          OR s.country_name ILIKE '%Italy%'
          OR s.country_name ILIKE '%Belgium%'
          OR s.location ILIKE '%Baku%'
          OR s.location ILIKE '%Monza%'
          OR s.location ILIKE '%Spa%'
      )
),
straight_line AS (
    SELECT
        sld.session_key,
        sld.driver_number,
        sld.driver_name,
        sld.team_name,
        sld.avg_speed_kph,
        sld.st_speed_kph,
        sld.i1_speed_kph,
        sld.i2_speed_kph
    FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
    WHERE sld.session_key IN (SELECT session_key FROM target_sessions)
      AND (
          sld.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%'
          OR sld.team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%'
      )
),
aggregated AS (
    SELECT
        ts.circuit_short_name,
        ts.country_name,
        ts.year,
        sl.team_name,
        ROUND(AVG(sl.avg_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_speed_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(sl.st_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_st_speed_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(sl.i1_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_i1_speed_kph,
        ROUND(AVG(sl.i2_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_i2_speed_kph,
        COUNT(DISTINCT sl.driver_number) AS driver_count
    FROM straight_line sl
    JOIN target_sessions ts ON ts.session_key = sl.session_key
    GROUP BY ts.circuit_short_name, ts.country_name, ts.year, sl.team_name
),
pivoted AS (
    SELECT
        a.circuit_short_name,
        a.country_name,
        a.year,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%' THEN a.avg_speed_kph END) AS mclaren_avg_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%' THEN a.avg_speed_kph END) AS redbull_avg_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%' THEN a.avg_st_speed_kph END) AS mclaren_st_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%' THEN a.avg_st_speed_kph END) AS redbull_st_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%' THEN a.avg_i1_speed_kph END) AS mclaren_i1_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%' THEN a.avg_i1_speed_kph END) AS redbull_i1_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%McLaren%' THEN a.avg_i2_speed_kph END) AS mclaren_i2_speed,
        MAX(CASE WHEN a.team_name ILIKE '%Red Bull%' THEN a.avg_i2_speed_kph END) AS redbull_i2_speed
    FROM aggregated a
    GROUP BY a.circuit_short_name, a.country_name, a.year
)
SELECT
    p.circuit_short_name,
    p.country_name,
    p.year,
    p.mclaren_avg_speed,
    p.redbull_avg_speed,
    ROUND((p.redbull_avg_speed - p.mclaren_avg_speed)::numeric, 2) AS redbull_vs_mclaren_avg_delta_kph,
    p.mclaren_st_speed,
    p.redbull_st_speed,
    ROUND((p.redbull_st_speed - p.mclaren_st_speed)::numeric, 2) AS redbull_vs_mclaren_st_delta_kph,
    p.mclaren_i1_speed,
    p.redbull_i1_speed,
    ROUND((p.redbull_i1_speed - p.mclaren_i1_speed)::numeric, 2) AS redbull_vs_mclaren_i1_delta_kph,
    p.mclaren_i2_speed,
    p.redbull_i2_speed,
    ROUND((p.redbull_i2_speed - p.mclaren_i2_speed)::numeric, 2) AS redbull_vs_mclaren_i2_delta_kph
FROM pivoted p
ORDER BY p.year, p.circuit_short_name

The best available proxy for 'early throttle application' is analytics.traction_analysis, which captures exit_speed_kph, avg_exit_throttle_pct, and exit_throttle_application_pct per corner per driver. I join it with analytics.corner_analysis (which holds per-lap entry/apex/exit speeds) on the same session_key, driver_number, and corner_id so we also see the speed profile. The corner is matched with ILIKE '%Degner 1%' against the canonical Suzuka label 'Turn 8 (Degner 1)'. Results are ordered by lap_number so we can see lap-by-lap throttle application consistency. | session_pin_verified(session_key=10006)
results.sql:
SELECT
  ca.lap_number,
  ca.corner_label,
  ca.entry_speed_kph,
  ca.apex_min_speed_kph,
  ca.exit_speed_kph,
  ca.sample_count,
  ta.exit_speed_kph          AS traction_exit_speed_kph,
  ta.avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  ta.exit_throttle_application_pct
FROM analytics.corner_analysis ca
LEFT JOIN analytics.traction_analysis ta
  ON  ta.session_key   = ca.session_key
  AND ta.driver_number = ca.driver_number
  AND ta.corner_id     = ca.corner_id
WHERE ca.session_key   = 10006
  AND ca.driver_number = 81
  AND ca.corner_label  ILIKE '%Degner 1%'
ORDER BY ca.lap_number
LIMIT 200
