results.question: Compare Verstappen and Piastri exit speed through 130R at Suzuka 2025 — where did the difference open up before the chicane?
results.author_note: RaceFans Suzuka super-clipping analysis on 130R coast pace.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At 130R itself, Verstappen carried a significantly higher apex speed (240.0 vs 234.9 kph, +5.1 kph) despite entering slightly slower than Piastri (288.7 vs 291.3 kph, -2.6 kph), resulting in a 2.6 kph exit speed advantage for Verstappen (247.6 vs 245.1 kph). However, the gap actually opened up earlier in the sequence: at the Esses (Turn 7), Verstappen was already 5.7 kph faster on exit (215.1 vs 209.4 kph), and he maintained a ~3.7 kph exit advantage through Degner 2 (Turn 9). By contrast, the Degner 1 section (Turn 8) was essentially equal between the two drivers (~0.7 kph differences throughout). So the meaningful speed differential before the chicane was built primarily at the Esses and sustained through Degner 2, with Verstappen's superior 130R apex commitment adding a further advantage into the chicane braking zone.
results.answerReasoning: The four rows cover Turns 7 (Esses), 8 (Degner 1), 9 (Degner 2), and 11 (130R). Exit deltas show the largest gap at Turn 7 (+5.65 kph VER), a near-zero gap at Turn 8 (+0.70 kph), a moderate gap at Turn 9 (+3.68 kph), and then at 130R Verstappen's apex advantage (+5.11 kph) translates to a +2.58 kph exit lead. This progression clearly shows the difference opening at the Esses and being the dominant factor before the chicane.
results.generationNotes: The question asks where the speed difference between Verstappen (driver 1) and Piastri (driver 81) opens up leading into the chicane at Suzuka 2025 (session_key 10006). The relevant corners in sequence before the chicane are the Esses (Turn 7), Degner 1 & 2 (Turns 8-9), and 130R itself. I use analytics.corner_analysis filtered to those corners for both drivers, aggregate entry/apex/exit speeds per corner across all valid laps, then pivot to show side-by-side deltas (VER minus PIA) at each phase of each corner. This reveals exactly where in the sequence the gap opens — whether it's at 130R entry, apex, or exit, or whether it traces back to the Degner complex. | session_pin_verified(session_key=10006)
results.sql:
WITH corners_of_interest AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    lap_number,
    corner_label,
    corner_number,
    entry_speed_kph,
    apex_min_speed_kph,
    exit_speed_kph,
    sample_count
  FROM analytics.corner_analysis
  WHERE session_key = 10006
    AND driver_number IN (1, 81)
    AND corner_label ILIKE ANY(ARRAY['%130R%', '%Degner%', '%Esses%'])
),
agg AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    corner_number,
    corner_label,
    ROUND(AVG(entry_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS avg_entry_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(apex_min_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_apex_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)     AS avg_exit_speed_kph,
    COUNT(*)                                   AS lap_samples
  FROM corners_of_interest
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, corner_number, corner_label
),
pivoted AS (
  SELECT
    corner_number,
    corner_label,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_entry_speed_kph END) AS ver_entry,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_entry_speed_kph END) AS pia_entry,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_apex_speed_kph  END) AS ver_apex,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_apex_speed_kph  END) AS pia_apex,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_exit_speed_kph  END) AS ver_exit,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_exit_speed_kph  END) AS pia_exit,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN lap_samples         END) AS ver_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN lap_samples         END) AS pia_laps
  FROM agg
  GROUP BY corner_number, corner_label
)
SELECT
  corner_number,
  corner_label,
  ver_entry,
  pia_entry,
  ROUND((ver_entry - pia_entry)::numeric, 2) AS entry_delta_ver_minus_pia,
  ver_apex,
  pia_apex,
  ROUND((ver_apex - pia_apex)::numeric, 2)   AS apex_delta_ver_minus_pia,
  ver_exit,
  pia_exit,
  ROUND((ver_exit - pia_exit)::numeric, 2)   AS exit_delta_ver_minus_pia,
  ver_laps,
  pia_laps
FROM pivoted
ORDER BY corner_number ASC

results.question: Did Leclerc's rear-end instability at Imola Turn 3 in 2025 cost him more on traction-out than on entry vs Piastri?
results.author_note: PlanetF1 Imola Ferrari upgrades analysis (T3 exit as Leclerc's loss point).
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data does not clearly support the premise that Leclerc suffered more on traction-out than on entry at Imola Turn 3 versus Piastri. In fact, Leclerc's biggest deficit was on entry speed (-20.35 kph vs Piastri), while his exit/traction deficit was smaller (-3.59 kph on avg exit speed, -6.00 kph on traction exit speed). Interestingly, Leclerc actually approached Turn 3 faster (+8.00 kph approach speed) and braked harder (+9.00 kph speed drop), yet arrived at the corner entry significantly slower (-20.35 kph), suggesting the instability manifested most severely during the braking/entry phase. On the traction side, Leclerc actually applied throttle slightly earlier and more aggressively than Piastri (exit throttle +1.98%, throttle application +1.82%), meaning the exit phase was not where he lost the most ground.
results.answerReasoning: The entry_speed_delta_kph of -20.35 kph is the largest deficit Leclerc shows versus Piastri, despite a higher approach speed (+8 kph) and bigger brake zone drop (+9 kph), indicating the instability cost him most during the braking/entry phase. The traction exit speed delta is -6.00 kph and avg exit speed delta is -3.59 kph, which are meaningful but smaller than the entry deficit. Leclerc's throttle metrics are actually slightly better than Piastri's on exit, further undermining the claim that traction-out was his primary weakness.
results.generationNotes: The question asks whether Leclerc's rear-end instability at Imola Turn 3 cost him more on traction-out (exit phase) than on entry vs Piastri. I use analytics.corner_analysis for entry/apex/exit speed, analytics.traction_analysis for exit throttle application and exit speed under traction, and analytics.braking_performance for entry/braking-zone metrics — all filtered to session_key=9987, driver_numbers 16 and 81, corner_number=3. I then compute deltas (Leclerc minus Piastri) for both the entry phase (approach speed, brake zone drop, entry speed) and the traction/exit phase (exit speed, traction exit speed, throttle application %). A larger negative delta on the exit/traction metrics vs the entry metrics would confirm the instability cost more on traction-out. The result is a single comparison row with all relevant deltas and raw values for synthesis. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)
results.sql:
WITH corner_stats AS (
  SELECT
    ca.driver_number,
    ca.driver_name,
    ca.corner_number,
    ca.corner_label,
    ROUND(AVG(ca.entry_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)       AS avg_entry_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(ca.apex_min_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS avg_apex_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(ca.exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)        AS avg_exit_speed_kph,
    COUNT(*)                                          AS lap_samples
  FROM analytics.corner_analysis ca
  WHERE ca.session_key = 9987
    AND ca.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND ca.corner_number = 3
  GROUP BY ca.driver_number, ca.driver_name, ca.corner_number, ca.corner_label
),
traction_stats AS (
  SELECT
    ta.driver_number,
    ta.driver_name,
    ta.corner_number,
    ta.corner_label,
    ROUND(ta.exit_speed_kph::numeric, 2)             AS traction_exit_speed_kph,
    ROUND(ta.avg_exit_throttle_pct::numeric, 2)      AS avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    ROUND(ta.exit_throttle_application_pct::numeric, 2) AS exit_throttle_application_pct,
    ta.valid_lap_count
  FROM analytics.traction_analysis ta
  WHERE ta.session_key = 9987
    AND ta.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND ta.corner_number = 3
),
braking_stats AS (
  SELECT
    bp.driver_number,
    bp.driver_name,
    bp.corner_number,
    bp.corner_label,
    ROUND(bp.approach_speed_kph::numeric, 2)         AS approach_speed_kph,
    ROUND(bp.min_brake_zone_speed_kph::numeric, 2)   AS min_brake_zone_speed_kph,
    ROUND(bp.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph::numeric, 2)  AS brake_zone_speed_drop_kph,
    ROUND(bp.peak_brake_pressure_pct::numeric, 2)    AS peak_brake_pressure_pct,
    bp.valid_lap_count
  FROM analytics.braking_performance bp
  WHERE bp.session_key = 9987
    AND bp.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND bp.corner_number = 3
),
combined AS (
  SELECT
    cs.driver_number,
    cs.driver_name,
    cs.corner_number,
    cs.corner_label,
    cs.avg_entry_speed_kph,
    cs.avg_apex_speed_kph,
    cs.avg_exit_speed_kph,
    cs.lap_samples,
    ts.traction_exit_speed_kph,
    ts.avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    ts.exit_throttle_application_pct,
    ts.valid_lap_count                               AS traction_valid_laps,
    bs.approach_speed_kph,
    bs.min_brake_zone_speed_kph,
    bs.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph,
    bs.peak_brake_pressure_pct,
    bs.valid_lap_count                               AS braking_valid_laps
  FROM corner_stats cs
  LEFT JOIN traction_stats ts
    ON cs.driver_number = ts.driver_number AND cs.corner_number = ts.corner_number
  LEFT JOIN braking_stats bs
    ON cs.driver_number = bs.driver_number AND cs.corner_number = bs.corner_number
),
delta AS (
  SELECT
    c16.driver_name                                                          AS leclerc_name,
    c81.driver_name                                                          AS piastri_name,
    -- Entry phase delta (approach speed & brake zone)
    ROUND((c16.approach_speed_kph - c81.approach_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS entry_approach_speed_delta_kph,
    ROUND((c16.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph - c81.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph)::numeric, 2) AS brake_zone_drop_delta_kph,
    ROUND((c16.avg_entry_speed_kph - c81.avg_entry_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS entry_speed_delta_kph,
    -- Apex delta
    ROUND((c16.avg_apex_speed_kph - c81.avg_apex_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS apex_speed_delta_kph,
    -- Traction / exit phase delta
    ROUND((c16.avg_exit_speed_kph - c81.avg_exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS exit_speed_delta_kph,
    ROUND((c16.traction_exit_speed_kph - c81.traction_exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS traction_exit_speed_delta_kph,
    ROUND((c16.avg_exit_throttle_pct - c81.avg_exit_throttle_pct)::numeric, 2)     AS exit_throttle_delta_pct,
    ROUND((c16.exit_throttle_application_pct - c81.exit_throttle_application_pct)::numeric, 2) AS throttle_application_delta_pct,
    -- Raw values for context
    c16.avg_entry_speed_kph      AS lec_entry_kph,
    c81.avg_entry_speed_kph      AS pia_entry_kph,
    c16.avg_apex_speed_kph       AS lec_apex_kph,
    c81.avg_apex_speed_kph       AS pia_apex_kph,
    c16.avg_exit_speed_kph       AS lec_exit_kph,
    c81.avg_exit_speed_kph       AS pia_exit_kph,
    c16.traction_exit_speed_kph  AS lec_traction_exit_kph,
    c81.traction_exit_speed_kph  AS pia_traction_exit_kph,
    c16.avg_exit_throttle_pct    AS lec_exit_throttle_pct,
    c81.avg_exit_throttle_pct    AS pia_exit_throttle_pct,
    c16.exit_throttle_application_pct AS lec_throttle_app_pct,
    c81.exit_throttle_application_pct AS pia_throttle_app_pct,
    c16.approach_speed_kph       AS lec_approach_kph,
    c81.approach_speed_kph       AS pia_approach_kph,
    c16.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph AS lec_brake_drop_kph,
    c81.brake_zone_speed_drop_kph AS pia_brake_drop_kph,
    c16.lap_samples              AS lec_lap_samples,
    c81.lap_samples              AS pia_lap_samples
  FROM combined c16
  JOIN combined c81 ON c81.driver_number = 81
  WHERE c16.driver_number = 16
)
SELECT * FROM delta
LIMIT 1

results.question: Compare Russell and Hamilton throttle-on point at the final corner at Silverstone 2025 — given Copse went flat, where does the exit-traction phase actually decide the lap?
results.author_note: Formula1.com British GP coverage of Copse going flat — exit-traction phase decides the lap at Silverstone's Turn 18.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At Club Corner (Turn 18) in Silverstone 2025, Russell carries a significantly higher average apex speed (109.65 vs 99.35 kph, +10.3 kph) and exits faster on average (120.62 vs 113.67 kph, +6.95 kph), suggesting he applies throttle earlier and/or more aggressively out of the apex. Hamilton, however, shows a larger traction delta (speed gained from apex to exit: 14.33 vs 10.96 kph, +3.37 kph), meaning he accelerates harder once he does get on the power — but his later/lower throttle-on point means he never fully closes the gap to Russell's exit speed. With Copse now flat, Club is the last meaningful braking and traction zone on the lap, making Russell's ~7 kph exit-speed advantage directly decisive for the run onto the Hangar Straight and ultimately lap time.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show Russell with +10.3 kph avg apex speed and +6.95 kph avg exit speed over Hamilton at Club. Hamilton's traction_delta (exit minus apex) is 14.33 vs Russell's 10.96, meaning Hamilton gains more speed through the traction phase but starts from a lower base. The high stddev (~43 kph for both) reflects mixed data including non-racing laps, but the averages over 52 laps each are consistent. Since Copse is flat, Club is the final traction-critical corner, making Russell's exit speed advantage the key differentiator.
results.generationNotes: The question asks about the final corner at Silverstone (Turn 18 – Club) and specifically the exit-traction phase, comparing Russell (63) and Hamilton (44). The MATVIEW HINT confirms the canonical label is 'Turn 18 (Club)' and the primary table is analytics.corner_analysis. I filter session_key=9947 and both driver numbers (44=Hamilton, 63=Russell), match the corner with ILIKE '%Club%', then aggregate per driver: avg entry/apex/exit speeds, max exit speed, stddev of exit speed, and a traction_delta (exit minus apex) which proxies how aggressively each driver accelerates out of the apex — i.e. the throttle-on / traction phase. A cross-join of the two drivers' aggregates produces head-to-head deltas so the synthesis layer can directly compare who carries more speed off the apex and who has a larger traction delta (earlier/harder throttle application). | The only fix needed was in the 'drivers' CTE: core.session_drivers has no 'driver_name' column, but it does have 'full_name'. Aliasing 'full_name AS driver_name' in that CTE propagates the correct label through all downstream CTEs (club_corner, agg, delta) without any other changes. The rest of the query — corner_analysis join on Club corner, per-driver aggregation of entry/apex/exit speeds and traction delta, and cross-driver delta computation — remains intact. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9947)
results.sql:
WITH drivers AS (
  SELECT driver_number, full_name AS driver_name
  FROM core.session_drivers
  WHERE session_key = 9947
    AND driver_number IN (44, 63)
),
club_corner AS (
  SELECT
    ca.driver_number,
    d.driver_name,
    ca.corner_label,
    ca.corner_number,
    ca.lap_number,
    ca.entry_speed_kph,
    ca.apex_min_speed_kph,
    ca.exit_speed_kph,
    ca.sample_count
  FROM analytics.corner_analysis ca
  JOIN drivers d ON ca.driver_number = d.driver_number
  WHERE ca.session_key = 9947
    AND ca.driver_number IN (44, 63)
    AND ca.corner_label ILIKE '%Club%'
),
agg AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    corner_label,
    corner_number,
    COUNT(lap_number)                          AS lap_count,
    ROUND(AVG(entry_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)    AS avg_entry_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(apex_min_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS avg_apex_speed_kph,
    ROUND(AVG(exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)     AS avg_exit_speed_kph,
    ROUND(MAX(exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)     AS max_exit_speed_kph,
    ROUND(MIN(apex_min_speed_kph)::numeric, 2) AS min_apex_speed_kph,
    ROUND(STDDEV(exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)  AS stddev_exit_speed_kph,
    ROUND((AVG(exit_speed_kph) - AVG(apex_min_speed_kph))::numeric, 2) AS traction_delta_kph
  FROM club_corner
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, corner_label, corner_number
),
delta AS (
  SELECT
    a.*,
    ROUND((a.avg_exit_speed_kph  - b.avg_exit_speed_kph)::numeric,  2) AS exit_speed_vs_other_kph,
    ROUND((a.avg_apex_speed_kph  - b.avg_apex_speed_kph)::numeric,  2) AS apex_speed_vs_other_kph,
    ROUND((a.traction_delta_kph  - b.traction_delta_kph)::numeric,  2) AS traction_delta_vs_other_kph
  FROM agg a
  JOIN agg b
    ON  a.corner_label   = b.corner_label
    AND a.driver_number <> b.driver_number
)
SELECT
  driver_number,
  driver_name,
  corner_label,
  lap_count,
  avg_entry_speed_kph,
  avg_apex_speed_kph,
  avg_exit_speed_kph,
  max_exit_speed_kph,
  min_apex_speed_kph,
  stddev_exit_speed_kph,
  traction_delta_kph,
  exit_speed_vs_other_kph,
  apex_speed_vs_other_kph,
  traction_delta_vs_other_kph
FROM delta
ORDER BY driver_number

results.question: At Hungaroring 2025, who had cleaner traction out of Turn 1 and Turn 13 between Norris and Piastri once tyres degraded?
results.author_note: OverTake Hungaroring guide: T1 and T13 are tyre-sensitive on exit.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At Hungaroring 2025, Piastri had cleaner traction out of both corners as tyres degraded. Out of Turn 1, Piastri posted a significantly higher exit speed (329 km/h vs. 318 km/h) and applied throttle more aggressively (35.2% avg exit throttle vs. 30.5% for Norris). Out of Turn 13, exit speeds were identical (261 km/h each), but Piastri again showed higher throttle application (63.9% vs. 60.8%). Piastri also suffered slightly less tyre degradation (−0.041 s/lap fuel-corrected vs. −0.042 s/lap for Norris), consistent with his cleaner exits preserving rubber better across the stint.
results.answerReasoning: The rows directly show exit_speed_kph and avg_exit_throttle_pct for both drivers at corners 1 and 13. Piastri leads Norris on exit speed at Turn 1 by 11 km/h and on throttle application at both corners. The degradation figures (avg_fuel_corrected_deg_per_lap_s) show Piastri degrading marginally less, supporting the conclusion that his traction was cleaner as tyres wore.
results.generationNotes: The question asks about traction quality out of Turn 1 and Turn 13 at Hungaroring 2025 (session_key=9928) for Norris (driver 4) and Piastri (driver 81), specifically in the context of tyre degradation. I use analytics.traction_analysis filtered to session_key=9928, driver_numbers 4 and 81, and corner_numbers 1 and 13. The key traction metrics are exit_speed_kph, avg_exit_throttle_pct, and exit_throttle_application_pct — higher values indicate cleaner, more aggressive traction. I join analytics.stint_degradation_curve to provide degradation context (avg deg per lap and fuel-corrected deg per lap) so the comparison can be framed against how much each driver's tyres degraded. Results are ordered by corner then exit speed descending so the faster-exiting driver appears first per corner. | The only fix needed was in the 'drivers' CTE: core.session_drivers has no 'driver_name' column, but does have 'full_name'. Aliased 'full_name AS driver_name' so all downstream references to driver_name remain valid. All other logic (traction_analysis for corners 1 and 13, stint_degradation_curve for deg context, joining Norris=4 and Piastri=81) is unchanged. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH drivers AS (
  SELECT driver_number, full_name AS driver_name, team_name
  FROM core.session_drivers
  WHERE session_key = 9928
    AND driver_number IN (4, 81)
),
traction AS (
  SELECT
    ta.driver_number,
    d.driver_name,
    d.team_name,
    ta.corner_id,
    ta.corner_number,
    ta.corner_label,
    ta.exit_speed_kph,
    ta.avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    ta.exit_throttle_application_pct,
    ta.valid_lap_count,
    ta.sample_count
  FROM analytics.traction_analysis ta
  JOIN drivers d ON ta.driver_number = d.driver_number
  WHERE ta.session_key = 9928
    AND ta.driver_number IN (4, 81)
    AND ta.corner_number IN (1, 13)
),
deg_context AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    team_name,
    AVG(degradation_per_lap_s) AS avg_deg_per_lap,
    AVG(fuel_corrected_degradation_per_lap_s) AS avg_fuel_corrected_deg_per_lap
  FROM analytics.stint_degradation_curve
  WHERE session_key = 9928
    AND driver_number IN (4, 81)
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, team_name
)
SELECT
  t.driver_number,
  t.driver_name,
  t.team_name,
  t.corner_number,
  t.corner_label,
  ROUND(t.exit_speed_kph::numeric, 2)                    AS exit_speed_kph,
  ROUND(t.avg_exit_throttle_pct::numeric, 2)             AS avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  ROUND(t.exit_throttle_application_pct::numeric, 2)     AS exit_throttle_application_pct,
  t.valid_lap_count,
  t.sample_count,
  ROUND(dc.avg_deg_per_lap::numeric, 4)                  AS avg_deg_per_lap_s,
  ROUND(dc.avg_fuel_corrected_deg_per_lap::numeric, 4)   AS avg_fuel_corrected_deg_per_lap_s
FROM traction t
LEFT JOIN deg_context dc
  ON t.driver_number = dc.driver_number
ORDER BY t.corner_number, t.exit_speed_kph DESC

results.question: Across Suzuka's three heaviest traction zones (Spoon exit, 130R-out, chicane exit) at the 2025 Japanese GP, did Verstappen's lower-downforce setup recover all the cornering deficit through superior straight-line top speed vs Piastri's higher-downforce McLaren?
results.author_note: RaceFans super-clipping Suzuka piece on Verstappen recovering cornering deficit through straight-line speed.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: No, Verstappen's setup did not recover the cornering deficit through straight-line speed — in fact, Piastri's McLaren was faster in both areas. At 130R, Verstappen exited 3 km/h slower (289 vs 292 km/h) and at Degner 1 he was also 3 km/h down (294 vs 297 km/h). At the chicane exit Verstappen was marginally ahead by just 1 km/h (179 vs 178 km/h). Crucially, on the straights Piastri held a 2 km/h top-speed advantage (335 vs 333 km/h) and a 1.08 km/h average-speed advantage, meaning Verstappen had no straight-line edge to compensate for his corner-exit losses. The data shows Piastri's McLaren was quicker at traction zones and on the straights, leaving Verstappen's deficit unrecovered.
results.answerReasoning: The rows show Verstappen trailing Piastri at 130R exit (−3 km/h) and Degner 1 exit (−3 km/h), with only marginal gains at the chicane (+1 km/h) and Degner 2 (+1 km/h). On straights, Piastri leads by 2 km/h in top speed and 1.08 km/h in average speed. The recovery_assessment for the two key traction zones explicitly states 'VER slower at corner exit AND no straight-line advantage — deficit not recovered', confirming the setup trade-off did not work in Verstappen's favour.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks whether Verstappen's presumed lower-downforce setup recovered his traction-zone deficit vs Piastri through straight-line speed at the 2025 Japanese GP (session_key=10006, drivers 1 and 81).

Approach:
1. **Traction zones** – Pull analytics.traction_analysis for session 10006, drivers 1 & 81, filtering corner_label for Spoon, 130R, and chicane (plus Degner as a fallback label variant). This gives exit_speed_kph, avg_exit_throttle_pct, and exit_throttle_application_pct per corner per driver.
2. **Straight-line dominance** – Pull analytics.straight_line_dominance for the same session/drivers. st_speed_kph (MAX speed proxy), i2_speed_kph (95th pctile), and avg_speed_kph are statistical proxies across the whole session — no spatial zone attribution, cited as such.
3. **Pivot** – Pivot both datasets to VER vs PIA columns and compute deltas (ver_minus_pia_exit_speed, ver_minus_pia_top_speed, ver_minus_pia_avg_speed).
4. **Recovery assessment** – A CASE expression classifies whether the straight-line advantage (if any) plausibly offsets the corner-exit deficit, giving a per-corner qualitative verdict.

Limitations noted: straight_line_dominance is a session-level aggregate (no per-zone attribution); traction_analysis corner labels depend on f1.track_segments ingestion for Suzuka — if no rows match the ILIKE filters the result will be empty for the traction section. The recovery_assessment is a heuristic proxy, not a lap-time decomposition. | The error 'function round(double precision, integer) does not exist' occurs because PostgreSQL's ROUND(x, n) with two arguments only accepts numeric (not double precision) as the first argument. The fix is to cast the double precision expressions to ::numeric before passing them to ROUND(). All three ROUND() calls in corner_pivot and straight_pivot have been updated with explicit ::numeric casts on the subtraction expressions. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=10006)

results.sql:
WITH traction AS (
  SELECT
    ta.driver_number,
    ta.driver_name,
    ta.team_name,
    ta.corner_label,
    ta.exit_speed_kph,
    ta.avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    ta.exit_throttle_application_pct,
    ta.valid_lap_count,
    ta.sample_count
  FROM analytics.traction_analysis ta
  WHERE ta.session_key = 10006
    AND ta.driver_number IN (1, 81)
    AND (
      ta.corner_label ILIKE '%spoon%'
      OR ta.corner_label ILIKE '%130R%'
      OR ta.corner_label ILIKE '%chicane%'
      OR ta.corner_label ILIKE '%degner%'
    )
),
straight_line AS (
  SELECT
    sld.driver_number,
    sld.driver_name,
    sld.team_name,
    sld.st_speed_kph,
    sld.i2_speed_kph,
    sld.i1_speed_kph,
    sld.avg_speed_kph,
    sld.speed_sample_count
  FROM analytics.straight_line_dominance sld
  WHERE sld.session_key = 10006
    AND sld.driver_number IN (1, 81)
),
corner_pivot AS (
  SELECT
    corner_label,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN exit_speed_kph END)                AS ver_exit_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN exit_speed_kph END)                AS pia_exit_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_exit_throttle_pct END)         AS ver_avg_throttle,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_exit_throttle_pct END)         AS pia_avg_throttle,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN exit_throttle_application_pct END) AS ver_throttle_app_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN exit_throttle_application_pct END) AS pia_throttle_app_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN valid_lap_count END)               AS ver_valid_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN valid_lap_count END)               AS pia_valid_laps,
    ROUND(
      (MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN exit_speed_kph END)
      - MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN exit_speed_kph END))::numeric, 2
    )                                                                         AS ver_minus_pia_exit_speed
  FROM traction
  GROUP BY corner_label
),
straight_pivot AS (
  SELECT
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN st_speed_kph  END)  AS ver_top_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN st_speed_kph  END)  AS pia_top_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN i2_speed_kph  END)  AS ver_p95_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN i2_speed_kph  END)  AS pia_p95_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_speed_kph END)  AS ver_avg_speed,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_speed_kph END)  AS pia_avg_speed,
    ROUND(
      (MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN st_speed_kph END)
      - MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN st_speed_kph END))::numeric, 2
    )                                                          AS ver_minus_pia_top_speed,
    ROUND(
      (MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 1  THEN avg_speed_kph END)
      - MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_speed_kph END))::numeric, 2
    )                                                          AS ver_minus_pia_avg_speed
  FROM straight_line
)
SELECT
  cp.corner_label,
  cp.ver_exit_speed,
  cp.pia_exit_speed,
  cp.ver_minus_pia_exit_speed                                  AS corner_exit_delta_kph,
  cp.ver_avg_throttle,
  cp.pia_avg_throttle,
  cp.ver_throttle_app_pct,
  cp.pia_throttle_app_pct,
  cp.ver_valid_laps,
  cp.pia_valid_laps,
  sp.ver_top_speed,
  sp.pia_top_speed,
  sp.ver_minus_pia_top_speed                                   AS straight_top_speed_delta_kph,
  sp.ver_p95_speed,
  sp.pia_p95_speed,
  sp.ver_avg_speed,
  sp.pia_avg_speed,
  sp.ver_minus_pia_avg_speed                                   AS straight_avg_speed_delta_kph,
  CASE
    WHEN sp.ver_minus_pia_top_speed > 0
         AND cp.ver_minus_pia_exit_speed < 0
    THEN 'VER gains on straights but loses at corner exit — partial recovery possible'
    WHEN sp.ver_minus_pia_top_speed > 0
         AND cp.ver_minus_pia_exit_speed >= 0
    THEN 'VER faster both on straights AND at corner exit — no deficit to recover'
    WHEN sp.ver_minus_pia_top_speed <= 0
         AND cp.ver_minus_pia_exit_speed < 0
    THEN 'VER slower at corner exit AND no straight-line advantage — deficit not recovered'
    ELSE 'Mixed picture — further lap-time analysis required'
  END                                                          AS recovery_assessment
FROM corner_pivot cp
CROSS JOIN straight_pivot sp
ORDER BY cp.corner_label

results.question: Across Turn 3 and Turn 9 exits at Imola 2025, quantify Leclerc's full-throttle-application delay vs Piastri across his stint to gauge whether tyre wear amplified the deficit.
results.author_note: PlanetF1 Imola Ferrari weakness analysis (T3, T9 'rear-limited, unstable rear end' corners). Cross-table HIGH.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At Imola 2025, Leclerc's full-throttle application pattern versus Piastri differs by corner. At Turn 3 (Tamburello), Leclerc actually applied throttle slightly earlier/more aggressively (71.32% vs Piastri's 69.50%, a +1.81pp delta in Leclerc's favour), yet his exit speed was 6 km/h lower (276 vs 282 km/h), suggesting he was getting on throttle sooner but with less traction effect. At Turn 9 (Acque Minerali), the throttle application rates were nearly identical (69.29% vs 69.49%, a negligible -0.20pp gap), and Leclerc actually held a 7 km/h exit speed advantage there. Regarding tyre wear amplification, Leclerc's own exit speeds at Turn 3 dropped from 209 km/h early-stint to 187 km/h mid-stint and stayed there late-stint, while apex speeds fell from 148 to 132 km/h — a clear degradation signal. At Turn 9 the pattern was less severe (217 → 200 → 208 km/h exit, apex 189 → 180 km/h). Overall, the data does not show Leclerc suffering a widening throttle-application delay versus Piastri as tyres aged; the deficit at Turn 3 appears structural (exit speed gap despite earlier throttle), while tyre wear did reduce Leclerc's own corner speeds meaningfully, particularly at Tamburello.
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide session-aggregate throttle application percentages and exit speeds for both drivers, plus Leclerc's per-stint-age exit and apex speeds. The throttle_app_delta shows Leclerc is +1.81pp at T3 and -0.20pp at T9 vs Piastri — no meaningful delay. However, Leclerc's exit speed is 6 km/h lower at T3 despite earlier throttle, and his own early-to-mid stint exit speed drops ~23 km/h at T3 and apex speed drops ~15 km/h, confirming tyre wear impact on his own performance but not a growing gap specifically in throttle application timing relative to Piastri.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks for Leclerc (driver 16) vs Piastri (driver 81) full-throttle-application delay at Turn 3 and Turn 9 exits at Imola 2025 (session_key=9987), with tyre-age progression context to see if wear amplified the deficit.

Approach:
1. Use `analytics.traction_analysis` as the primary contract — it has `exit_throttle_application_pct` (fraction of exit window where throttle is applied), `avg_exit_throttle_pct`, and `exit_speed_kph` per corner per driver per session. This directly captures throttle application behaviour at corner exits.
2. Filter to corner_number IN (3, 9) for Turn 3 and Turn 9, and both driver_numbers 16 and 81.
3. Pivot to produce a side-by-side comparison with deltas (Leclerc minus Piastri) for throttle application %, avg exit throttle %, and exit speed.
4. Join `analytics.corner_analysis` (which has per-lap corner metrics) with `core.laps_enriched` tyre_age_on_lap to bucket Leclerc's exit speed into early/mid/late stint bands — this reveals whether tyre wear degraded his corner exit performance over the stint.
5. The `session_laps` CTE filters both drivers' valid laps excluding pit laps for clean context.
6. A negative `throttle_app_delta_lec_minus_pia` means Leclerc applies full throttle later (delay) vs Piastri; combined with the tyre-age banding, we can see if the gap widens in late-stint laps. | The error was caused by referencing `sl.session_key` in the `lec_corner_by_tyre_age` CTE's JOIN condition, but `session_laps` CTE does not carry a `session_key` column (it was filtered out during the SELECT). The fix is to remove `sl.session_key = 9987` from the JOIN predicate in `lec_corner_by_tyre_age` — the session filter is already applied via `ca.session_key = 9987` in the WHERE clause and the `session_laps` CTE itself is already filtered to session 9987. The JOIN now correctly uses only `sl.driver_number = ca.driver_number AND sl.lap_number = ca.lap_number`. All other logic remains intact. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)
results.sql:
WITH session_laps AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.lap_start_ts,
    le.lap_end_ts,
    le.stint_number,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.compound_name,
    le.is_valid
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 9987
    AND le.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
    AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
    AND le.lap_number > 1
),
traction_data AS (
  SELECT
    ta.session_key,
    ta.driver_number,
    ta.driver_name,
    ta.team_name,
    ta.corner_id,
    ta.corner_number,
    ta.corner_label,
    ta.exit_speed_kph,
    ta.avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    ta.exit_throttle_application_pct,
    ta.valid_lap_count,
    ta.sample_count
  FROM analytics.traction_analysis ta
  WHERE ta.session_key = 9987
    AND ta.driver_number IN (16, 81)
    AND ta.corner_number IN (3, 9)
),
corner_comparison AS (
  SELECT
    corner_number,
    corner_label,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN exit_speed_kph END)                        AS lec_exit_speed_kph,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN exit_speed_kph END)                        AS pia_exit_speed_kph,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN avg_exit_throttle_pct END)                 AS lec_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN avg_exit_throttle_pct END)                 AS pia_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN exit_throttle_application_pct END)         AS lec_throttle_application_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN exit_throttle_application_pct END)         AS pia_throttle_application_pct,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN valid_lap_count END)                       AS lec_valid_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN valid_lap_count END)                       AS pia_valid_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 16 THEN sample_count END)                          AS lec_samples,
    MAX(CASE WHEN driver_number = 81 THEN sample_count END)                          AS pia_samples
  FROM traction_data
  GROUP BY corner_number, corner_label
),
lec_corner_by_tyre_age AS (
  SELECT
    ca.corner_number,
    ca.corner_label,
    sl.tyre_age_on_lap,
    sl.compound_name,
    sl.stint_number,
    ca.exit_speed_kph,
    ca.apex_min_speed_kph,
    ca.entry_speed_kph,
    ca.lap_number
  FROM analytics.corner_analysis ca
  JOIN session_laps sl
    ON sl.driver_number = ca.driver_number
   AND sl.lap_number = ca.lap_number
  WHERE ca.session_key = 9987
    AND ca.driver_number = 16
    AND ca.corner_number IN (3, 9)
)
SELECT
  cc.corner_number,
  cc.corner_label,
  ROUND(cc.lec_throttle_application_pct::numeric, 2)                                          AS lec_throttle_application_pct,
  ROUND(cc.pia_throttle_application_pct::numeric, 2)                                          AS pia_throttle_application_pct,
  ROUND((cc.lec_throttle_application_pct - cc.pia_throttle_application_pct)::numeric, 2)     AS throttle_app_delta_lec_minus_pia,
  ROUND(cc.lec_exit_speed_kph::numeric, 2)                                                    AS lec_exit_speed_kph,
  ROUND(cc.pia_exit_speed_kph::numeric, 2)                                                    AS pia_exit_speed_kph,
  ROUND((cc.lec_exit_speed_kph - cc.pia_exit_speed_kph)::numeric, 2)                         AS exit_speed_delta_lec_minus_pia,
  ROUND(cc.lec_avg_exit_throttle_pct::numeric, 2)                                             AS lec_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  ROUND(cc.pia_avg_exit_throttle_pct::numeric, 2)                                             AS pia_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  ROUND((cc.lec_avg_exit_throttle_pct - cc.pia_avg_exit_throttle_pct)::numeric, 2)           AS avg_throttle_delta_lec_minus_pia,
  cc.lec_valid_laps,
  cc.pia_valid_laps,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN lcta.tyre_age_on_lap <= 10 THEN lcta.exit_speed_kph END)::numeric, 2)  AS lec_exit_speed_early_stint,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN lcta.tyre_age_on_lap > 10 AND lcta.tyre_age_on_lap <= 20 THEN lcta.exit_speed_kph END)::numeric, 2) AS lec_exit_speed_mid_stint,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN lcta.tyre_age_on_lap > 20 THEN lcta.exit_speed_kph END)::numeric, 2)  AS lec_exit_speed_late_stint,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN lcta.tyre_age_on_lap <= 10 THEN lcta.apex_min_speed_kph END)::numeric, 2) AS lec_apex_speed_early_stint,
  ROUND(AVG(CASE WHEN lcta.tyre_age_on_lap > 20 THEN lcta.apex_min_speed_kph END)::numeric, 2) AS lec_apex_speed_late_stint,
  COUNT(DISTINCT lcta.lap_number) AS lec_corner_laps_sampled
FROM corner_comparison cc
LEFT JOIN lec_corner_by_tyre_age lcta
  ON lcta.corner_number = cc.corner_number
GROUP BY
  cc.corner_number,
  cc.corner_label,
  cc.lec_throttle_application_pct,
  cc.pia_throttle_application_pct,
  cc.lec_exit_speed_kph,
  cc.pia_exit_speed_kph,
  cc.lec_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  cc.pia_avg_exit_throttle_pct,
  cc.lec_valid_laps,
  cc.pia_valid_laps
ORDER BY cc.corner_number

results.question: How many laps did Sainz spend behind another car at Monza 2025 before his contact with Bearman?
results.author_note: F1.com/ESPN Monza 2025: Sainz lost time fighting Bearman before being tagged into a spin.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: According to the available data, Sainz spent 88 laps in traffic (behind another car) out of 106 total laps in the session at Monza 2025 (session key 9912). However, the data does not specify which laps occurred before his contact with Bearman, so it's not possible to isolate exactly how many traffic laps preceded that incident.

While this tells us the overall traffic lap count for the session, there is no timestamp or lap number breakpoint in the returned rows indicating when the Bearman contact occurred, making it impossible to determine how many of those 88 traffic laps came before the incident.

results.sql:
SELECT
    tap.traffic_laps,
    tap.clean_air_laps,
    tap.traffic_pace_s,
    tap.clean_air_pace_s,
    tap.traffic_pace_delta_s,
    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM core.laps_enriched WHERE session_key = 9912 AND driver_number = 55) AS total_laps_in_session
FROM analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace tap
WHERE tap.session_key = 9912
  AND tap.driver_number = 55
LIMIT 1

results.question: How did Piastri's lap pace drop while running in Verstappen's dirty air at Imola 2025 vs his clean-air pace once he pitted?
results.author_note: Mark Hughes Imola 2025: Piastri's deg accelerated specifically in Red Bull's wake.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Max VERSTAPPEN leads on average lap (85) versus Oscar PIASTRI (86), a gap of 1.
results.generationNotes: template=max_leclerc_lap_pace_summary | session_pin_verified(session_key=9987)
results.sql:
WITH lap_data AS (
          SELECT
            l.driver_number,
            COALESCE(l.driver_name, d.full_name) AS full_name,
            l.lap_number,
            l.lap_duration,
            l.duration_sector_1,
            l.duration_sector_2,
            l.duration_sector_3,
            l.is_pit_out_lap
          FROM core.laps_enriched l
          LEFT JOIN core.session_drivers d
            ON d.session_key = l.session_key
           AND d.driver_number = l.driver_number
          WHERE l.session_key = 9987
            AND l.driver_number IN (1, 81)
            AND l.lap_duration IS NOT NULL
            AND l.lap_duration > 0
            AND COALESCE(l.is_valid, TRUE) = TRUE
        )
        SELECT
          driver_number,
          full_name,
          COUNT(*) AS total_laps,
          ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap,
          ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap,
          ROUND(percentile_cont(0.5) WITHIN GROUP (ORDER BY lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS median_lap,
          ROUND(STDDEV_POP(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS lap_stddev
        FROM lap_data
        GROUP BY driver_number, full_name
        ORDER BY avg_lap ASC

results.question: What was Verstappen's clean-air pace advantage over Russell once Russell came out behind him at Spa 2025 after the first stops?
results.author_note: Wikipedia Spa 2025 recap: Russell didn't have the pace to stick with Verstappen in clean traffic-free running.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Based on session-wide traffic-adjusted pace data for Spa 2025 (session 9939), Verstappen's clean-air pace was approximately 106.70s per lap versus Russell's 108.91s — a gap of 2.212 seconds per lap in Verstappen's favour. However, the data does not isolate the specific post-first-stop window when Russell emerged behind Verstappen; these figures represent overall clean-air averages across the race (Verstappen over 36 clean-air laps, Russell over 54). The query cannot confirm the exact pace advantage during that specific stint without lap-by-lap positional filtering.
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide session-wide clean_air_pace_s for both drivers and a computed gap of 2.212s. There is no lap-range or stint-specific breakdown showing only the laps after the first stops when Russell was directly behind Verstappen, so the answer uses the available aggregate while noting the limitation.
results.generationNotes: The question asks for Verstappen's clean-air pace advantage over Russell at Spa 2025 (session_key 9939) after the first stops, when Russell was running in traffic behind Verstappen. analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace provides per-driver, per-session clean_air_pace_s and traffic_pace_s. I pull both drivers (Verstappen = 1, Russell = 63), then compute: (a) the clean-air pace gap between them (Russell minus Verstappen, positive = Verstappen faster), and (b) Russell's traffic_pace_delta_s which captures how much time Russell lost running in traffic (i.e., behind Verstappen). This gives a complete picture of Verstappen's pace advantage in clean air vs. the additional cost Russell incurred in dirty air. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9939)
results.sql:
WITH drivers AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    team_name,
    clean_air_pace_s,
    traffic_pace_s,
    traffic_pace_delta_s,
    clean_air_laps,
    traffic_laps
  FROM analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace
  WHERE session_key = 9939
    AND driver_number IN (1, 63)
)
SELECT
  v.driver_name                                        AS verstappen_name,
  v.clean_air_pace_s                                   AS verstappen_clean_air_pace_s,
  v.traffic_pace_s                                     AS verstappen_traffic_pace_s,
  v.clean_air_laps                                     AS verstappen_clean_air_laps,
  r.driver_name                                        AS russell_name,
  r.clean_air_pace_s                                   AS russell_clean_air_pace_s,
  r.traffic_pace_s                                     AS russell_traffic_pace_s,
  r.traffic_pace_delta_s                               AS russell_traffic_pace_delta_s,
  r.traffic_laps                                       AS russell_traffic_laps,
  r.clean_air_laps                                     AS russell_clean_air_laps,
  ROUND((r.clean_air_pace_s - v.clean_air_pace_s)::numeric, 3) AS clean_air_pace_gap_s,
  ROUND(r.traffic_pace_delta_s::numeric, 3)            AS russell_traffic_cost_s
FROM drivers v
JOIN drivers r ON r.driver_number = 63
WHERE v.driver_number = 1

results.question: How many laps did Piastri spend in traffic behind Leclerc during his second stint at Hungary 2025?
results.author_note: ESPN/The Race Hungary recap: Piastri bottled up behind Leclerc for the first two stints.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: During Piastri's second stint at Hungary 2025 (laps 19–45, 27 laps total), he spent 4 laps directly behind Leclerc (i.e., one position behind him on track). However, the data shows those 4 laps are all listed as lap 20, suggesting a data duplication issue, so the reliable takeaway is that he was in traffic behind Leclerc for approximately 1 distinct lap (lap 20) during that stint.
results.answerReasoning: The query counts laps where Piastri's position equals Leclerc's position + 1 (directly behind). It returns 4 such laps, but the laps_in_traffic_list shows '20, 20, 20, 20' — all the same lap number repeated four times, indicating a join duplication artifact rather than 4 distinct laps. The stint ran from lap 19 to lap 45 (27 laps). The only distinct lap where Piastri was directly behind Leclerc appears to be lap 20.
results.generationNotes: The question asks how many laps Piastri (driver 81) spent in traffic directly behind Leclerc (driver 16) during Piastri's second stint at Hungary 2025 (session_key 9928). I use core.stint_summary to identify Piastri's second stint lap range, then join core.race_progression_summary for both drivers to compare their positions lap by lap. A lap is counted as 'in traffic behind Leclerc' when Piastri's position_end_of_lap equals Leclerc's position_end_of_lap + 1 (i.e., Piastri is directly behind Leclerc). Note: the runtime context resolved driver_number 16 as Leclerc, but Piastri's driver number is 81 — the context filter of 16 refers to Leclerc as the car being followed. | The original query timed out likely due to using core.race_progression_summary which may be expensive. Replaced with core.laps_enriched which has position_end_of_lap and is indexed by session_key/driver_number. Used generate_series on the stint lap range to define stint 2 boundaries, then joined Piastri and Leclerc positions from laps_enriched. Traffic is defined as Piastri being exactly one position behind Leclerc (piastri_position = leclerc_position + 1). This avoids any cross-join or timestamp proximity join and should be fast. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH piastri_stint2 AS (
    SELECT lap_start, lap_end
    FROM core.stint_summary
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 81
      AND stint_number = 2
),
lap_range AS (
    SELECT generate_series(lap_start, lap_end) AS lap_number
    FROM piastri_stint2
),
piastri_pos AS (
    SELECT lap_number, position_end_of_lap AS piastri_position
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 81
),
leclerc_pos AS (
    SELECT lap_number, position_end_of_lap AS leclerc_position
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 16
),
stint2_laps AS (
    SELECT
        lr.lap_number,
        pp.piastri_position,
        lp.leclerc_position
    FROM lap_range lr
    JOIN piastri_pos pp ON lr.lap_number = pp.lap_number
    JOIN leclerc_pos lp ON lr.lap_number = lp.lap_number
)
SELECT
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE piastri_position = leclerc_position + 1) AS laps_directly_behind_leclerc,
    COUNT(*) AS total_stint2_laps,
    MIN(lap_number) AS stint2_first_lap,
    MAX(lap_number) AS stint2_last_lap,
    STRING_AGG(lap_number::text, ', ' ORDER BY lap_number)
        FILTER (WHERE piastri_position = leclerc_position + 1) AS laps_in_traffic_list
FROM stint2_laps

results.question: Compute Verstappen's traffic-corrected pace at Singapore 2025: how much lap time did he lose in his second stint when fighting other cars vs his predicted clean-air medium pace?
results.author_note: Verstappen Singapore quotes: mediums overheated due to traffic and fighting other cars.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: In Verstappen's second stint at Singapore 2025, he ran on the Hard compound (laps 21–62). The traffic model identifies 6 laps where he was in traffic, with an average traffic pace of 100.439s versus a clean-air pace of 96.656s — a delta of 3.783s per traffic lap, totalling an estimated 22.7 seconds lost to traffic. His overall average lap of 96.354s was 0.618s slower than his fuel-adjusted clean-air medium baseline of 95.736s across the stint.
results.answerReasoning: The row shows compound_name = HARD (not Medium as the question assumes), stint laps 21–62, traffic_pace_delta_s = 3.783s over 6 traffic laps giving 22.697s total time lost. The avg_vs_clean_air_delta_s of 0.618s reflects the broader stint average vs the fuel-adjusted baseline. The traffic model's clean_air_pace_s (96.656s) and traffic_pace_s (100.439s) directly quantify the traffic penalty. Note the compound is Hard, not Medium — the question's premise about 'medium pace' does not match the data.
results.generationNotes: The query focuses on Verstappen (driver_number=1) at Singapore 2025 (session_key=9896). It pulls his second stint valid laps from core.laps_enriched to compute average and best lap times, then joins analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace for the session-level traffic vs clean-air pace model (traffic_pace_s, clean_air_pace_s, traffic_pace_delta_s, traffic_laps, clean_air_laps). A fuel-adjusted clean-air medium baseline is computed from his own stint 2 laps using fuel_adj_lap_time. The output shows: (1) stint 2 lap range and compound, (2) raw avg/best lap, (3) predicted clean-air pace from both the fuel-adjusted model and the traffic model, (4) the traffic pace delta per lap, (5) total estimated time lost to traffic across traffic laps, and (6) the delta between his actual average and the clean-air prediction. This gives a complete picture of how much lap time he surrendered fighting traffic in his second stint versus his predicted clean-air medium pace. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9896)
results.sql:
WITH verstappen_stint2 AS (
    SELECT
        le.lap_number,
        le.lap_duration,
        le.fuel_adj_lap_time,
        le.tyre_age_on_lap,
        le.compound_name,
        le.stint_number,
        le.is_valid,
        le.is_pit_out_lap,
        le.is_pit_lap,
        le.position_end_of_lap
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9896
      AND le.driver_number = 1
      AND le.stint_number = 2
      AND le.is_valid = TRUE
      AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
),
traffic_pace AS (
    SELECT
        tap.traffic_laps,
        tap.clean_air_laps,
        tap.traffic_pace_s,
        tap.clean_air_pace_s,
        tap.traffic_pace_delta_s
    FROM analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace tap
    WHERE tap.session_key = 9896
      AND tap.driver_number = 1
),
stint2_summary AS (
    SELECT
        COUNT(*) AS total_valid_laps,
        MIN(lap_number) AS stint_start_lap,
        MAX(lap_number) AS stint_end_lap,
        AVG(lap_duration) AS avg_lap_s,
        MIN(lap_duration) AS best_lap_s,
        compound_name
    FROM verstappen_stint2
    GROUP BY compound_name
),
clean_air_medium_baseline AS (
    SELECT
        AVG(le.fuel_adj_lap_time) AS predicted_clean_air_medium_pace_s
    FROM core.laps_enriched le
    WHERE le.session_key = 9896
      AND le.driver_number = 1
      AND le.stint_number = 2
      AND le.is_valid = TRUE
      AND le.is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND le.fuel_adj_lap_time IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
    s2.compound_name,
    s2.stint_start_lap,
    s2.stint_end_lap,
    s2.total_valid_laps,
    ROUND(s2.avg_lap_s::numeric, 3)                          AS avg_lap_s,
    ROUND(s2.best_lap_s::numeric, 3)                         AS best_lap_s,
    ROUND(cab.predicted_clean_air_medium_pace_s::numeric, 3) AS predicted_clean_air_pace_s,
    ROUND(tp.clean_air_pace_s::numeric, 3)                   AS traffic_model_clean_air_pace_s,
    ROUND(tp.traffic_pace_s::numeric, 3)                     AS traffic_model_traffic_pace_s,
    ROUND(tp.traffic_pace_delta_s::numeric, 3)               AS traffic_pace_delta_s,
    tp.traffic_laps,
    tp.clean_air_laps,
    ROUND((s2.avg_lap_s - cab.predicted_clean_air_medium_pace_s)::numeric, 3) AS avg_vs_clean_air_delta_s,
    ROUND((tp.traffic_pace_delta_s * tp.traffic_laps)::numeric, 3)            AS estimated_total_time_lost_to_traffic_s
FROM stint2_summary s2
CROSS JOIN traffic_pace tp
CROSS JOIN clean_air_medium_baseline cab
LIMIT 10

results.question: What was Norris's clean-air pace delta to the field once he cleared the train of cars and took the lead at Hungary 2025?
results.author_note: RaceFans/ESPN Hungary: Norris's victory hinged on McLaren's true performance advantage once unblocked.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: The data only shows Norris leading (P1) on two laps — lap 20 on Mediums (tyre age 19) and lap 46 on Hards (tyre age 14) — which are likely duplicated rows rather than a continuous clean-air stint. On lap 20, Norris lapped 1.361s faster than the average field and 0.360s slower than the field's best lap (81.440s vs 82.801s avg / 81.080s best). On lap 46, he was 1.071s faster than the average field and 0.766s slower than the field's best (80.377s vs 81.448s avg / 79.611s best). His session-wide clean-air pace was 80.81s, roughly 0.75s faster than his traffic pace of 81.56s. However, the query only captures two laps where he held P1, so a full lap-by-lap clean-air pace delta across his entire lead stint is not available from these rows.
results.answerReasoning: The query returns only 4 rows (duplicated pairs for lap 20 and lap 46) where Norris was in P1. The delta_to_avg_field_s and delta_to_best_field_s columns directly show his pace advantage/disadvantage versus the field on those laps. The session-level clean_air_pace_s and traffic_pace_s from analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace provide the broader context. The limited lap coverage means we cannot characterize his full clean-air stint after taking the lead.
results.generationNotes: The question asks for Norris's clean-air pace delta to the field once he took the lead at Hungary 2025 (session_key=9928, driver_number=4). I filter core.laps_enriched for Norris's valid, non-pit laps where he was in P1 (position_end_of_lap=1) to capture his clean-air running at the front. I then compute the average and best lap times for the rest of the field on those same lap numbers (also valid, non-pit laps) to derive the delta. I also pull analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace for the session-level clean-air vs traffic pace summary for Norris, which gives a holistic view of his pace advantage once in clean air versus when stuck in traffic. | session_pin_verified(session_key=9928)
results.sql:
WITH norris_clean AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        lap_duration,
        position_end_of_lap,
        tyre_age_on_lap,
        compound_name
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 4
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
      AND position_end_of_lap = 1
),
field_clean AS (
    SELECT
        lap_number,
        AVG(lap_duration) AS avg_field_lap,
        MIN(lap_duration) AS best_field_lap,
        COUNT(DISTINCT driver_number) AS driver_count
    FROM core.laps_enriched
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number != 4
      AND is_valid = TRUE
      AND is_pit_out_lap = FALSE
      AND is_pit_lap = FALSE
    GROUP BY lap_number
),
traffic AS (
    SELECT
        clean_air_pace_s,
        traffic_pace_s,
        traffic_pace_delta_s,
        clean_air_laps,
        traffic_laps
    FROM analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace
    WHERE session_key = 9928
      AND driver_number = 4
)
SELECT
    n.lap_number,
    n.position_end_of_lap,
    n.compound_name,
    n.tyre_age_on_lap,
    ROUND(n.lap_duration::numeric, 3)                          AS norris_lap_s,
    ROUND(f.avg_field_lap::numeric, 3)                         AS avg_field_lap_s,
    ROUND(f.best_field_lap::numeric, 3)                        AS best_field_lap_s,
    ROUND((n.lap_duration - f.avg_field_lap)::numeric, 3)      AS delta_to_avg_field_s,
    ROUND((n.lap_duration - f.best_field_lap)::numeric, 3)     AS delta_to_best_field_s,
    f.driver_count                                             AS field_drivers_counted,
    t.clean_air_pace_s                                         AS session_clean_air_pace_s,
    t.traffic_pace_s                                           AS session_traffic_pace_s,
    t.traffic_pace_delta_s                                     AS session_traffic_delta_s,
    t.clean_air_laps                                           AS total_clean_air_laps,
    t.traffic_laps                                             AS total_traffic_laps
FROM norris_clean n
JOIN field_clean f ON n.lap_number = f.lap_number
CROSS JOIN traffic t
ORDER BY n.lap_number
LIMIT 100

results.sql:
SELECT
  tap.driver_number,
  tap.driver_name,
  tap.team_name,
  tap.traffic_laps,
  tap.clean_air_laps,
  ROUND(tap.traffic_pace_s::numeric, 3)       AS traffic_pace_s,
  ROUND(tap.clean_air_pace_s::numeric, 3)     AS clean_air_pace_s,
  ROUND(tap.traffic_pace_delta_s::numeric, 3) AS traffic_pace_delta_s,
  gvf.grid_position,
  gvf.finish_position,
  gvf.positions_gained
FROM analytics.traffic_adjusted_pace tap
LEFT JOIN core.grid_vs_finish gvf
  ON gvf.session_key = 9939
 AND gvf.driver_number = tap.driver_number
WHERE tap.session_key = 9939
  AND tap.driver_number = 44

results.question: What was the fresh-tyre warmup behaviour on the hard at Monza 2025?
results.author_note: f1technical Monza graining piece: only one driver did 17 laps on the hard, leaving teams with limited reference data.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Hard tyre warm-up behaviour at Monza 2025 varied enormously across drivers. The quickest to get the tyre up to speed were Alonso and Verstappen, both needing just 3 laps to reach their target pace (best laps of 83.8 s and 81.0 s respectively). Hamilton was next at 7 laps (81.5 s), followed by Russell (17 laps, 81.8 s) and Colapinto (19 laps, 82.2 s). At the other extreme, drivers who started on the hard from lap 1 — Gasly, Stroll, Ocon, and Albon — took 65–75 laps to reach their target, suggesting the hard compound was extremely slow to come in when used cold from the start of a race stint. Overall, the hard tyre showed a wide warm-up window of 3 to 75 laps, with mid-race starters generally warming up far faster than those who began the race on it.
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide warmup_laps_to_target for all 17 hard-tyre stints at session_key 9912 (Monza 2025). Sorting ascending reveals Alonso and Verstappen at 3 laps, Hamilton at 7, Russell at 17, up to Ocon at 75 laps. Drivers starting on the hard from lap 1 (Stroll, Gasly, Ocon, Albon, Hadjar) consistently show the highest warm-up lap counts (43–75), while those pitting onto the hard mid-race generally warmed up faster, indicating the compound is very sensitive to initial thermal conditions.
results.generationNotes: Using analytics.tyre_warmup as the primary contract for tyre warmup behaviour analysis. Norris's Medium stint ran laps 36–50 (15 laps), showing extremely low raw degradation of ~0.0015 s/lap (R² = 0.001, essentially flat/no meaningful linear trend), an average lap of 92.160 s, and a best of 91.778 s. Because Red Bull data is absent from the results, any claim about whether the gap between the two teams was aero-driven is unsupported by the available rows.

results.question: How did medium-vs-hard crossover lap differ between Verstappen and Piastri at Qatar 2025?
results.author_note: Coffee Corner Motorsport Qatar 2025 tyre piece: hard becomes faster than medium from lap 24.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: At Qatar 2025, both Verstappen and Piastri were faster on Hard than Medium, but the crossover gap differed significantly. Verstappen's Hard average was 6.5s quicker than his Medium average (84.4s vs 90.8s), with a best-lap delta of 3.3s (83.5s vs 86.8s) and a high Medium degradation of 1.33s/lap. Piastri showed an even larger average gap of 10.7s (83.5s vs 94.1s), though his best-lap delta was smaller at 2.1s (83.0s vs 85.1s), suggesting his Medium average was inflated by traffic or early-stint conditions rather than pure pace loss. Verstappen's Medium stint ran laps 8–32 (early race), while Piastri's ran laps 25–42 (mid-race), making direct comparison tricky, but on best-lap pace the compounds were closer for Piastri (2.1s gap) than Verstappen (3.3s gap).
results.answerReasoning: The rows provide avg and best lap times for Medium and Hard compounds for both drivers, along with degradation rates and stint lap ranges. The medium_vs_hard_avg_delta_s and medium_vs_hard_best_delta_s fields directly quantify the crossover difference. Verstappen's medium deg of 1.33s/lap vs Piastri's -0.37s/lap explains why Verstappen's average gap is smaller than Piastri's despite a larger best-lap delta.
results.generationNotes: The question asks how the medium-vs-hard crossover lap differed between Verstappen (driver 1) and Piastri (driver 81) at Qatar 2025 (session_key 9850). The MATVIEW HINT points to analytics.weather_impact for crossover_lap context, but that table's crossover_lap refers to wet/dry tyre crossovers (slick-to-inter), not medium-vs-hard compound crossovers. So I use core.laps_enriched as the primary source to compute per-compound pace (avg and best lap times, lap ranges) for MEDIUM and HARD compounds for both drivers, then compute the delta. I also pull the weather_impact crossover_lap for completeness. The compound pace delta and lap ranges reveal at which point in the race one compound became faster than the other, effectively showing the medium-vs-hard crossover dynamics for each driver. | The failing query incorrectly referenced 'tyre_age_on_lap' from analytics.weather_impact, which doesn't have that column. The weather_impact table's crossover_lap column refers to wet/dry crossover (slick-to-inter), not medium-vs-hard compound crossover — so it's not relevant here. Instead, I compute the medium-vs-hard crossover purely from core.laps_enriched: filtering valid, non-pit laps for each compound, computing per-compound averages and bests, then pivoting to compare. The delta (medium_avg - hard_avg) tells us which compound was faster on average and by how much, which is the meaningful 'crossover' metric for a dry race compound comparison. | auto_repair_applied | The original query timed out because it scanned core.laps_enriched row-by-row for session 9850. Instead, I use core.stint_summary which is a pre-aggregated table with one row per driver/stint, giving avg_valid_lap, best_valid_lap, and degradation_per_lap directly. This avoids the expensive full-table scan and aggregation on laps_enriched. The crossover logic (medium vs hard average and best lap delta, plus degradation rates) is preserved, and I filter to session_key=9850 and driver_numbers 1 and 81 on the small stint_summary table. | auto_repair_applied | session_pin_verified(session_key=9850)
results.sql:
WITH stint_data AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    team_name,
    compound_name,
    stint_number,
    lap_start,
    lap_end,
    stint_length_laps,
    valid_lap_count,
    avg_valid_lap AS avg_lap_s,
    best_valid_lap AS best_lap_s,
    degradation_per_lap
  FROM core.stint_summary
  WHERE session_key = 9850
    AND driver_number IN (1, 81)
    AND compound_name IN ('MEDIUM', 'HARD')
),
crossover_calc AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    team_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN avg_lap_s END) AS medium_avg_lap_s,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN avg_lap_s END) AS hard_avg_lap_s,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN best_lap_s END) AS medium_best_lap_s,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN best_lap_s END) AS hard_best_lap_s,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN valid_lap_count END) AS medium_valid_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN valid_lap_count END) AS hard_valid_laps,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN lap_start END) AS medium_first_lap,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN lap_end END)   AS medium_last_lap,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN lap_start END) AS hard_first_lap,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN lap_end END)   AS hard_last_lap,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'MEDIUM' THEN degradation_per_lap END) AS medium_deg_per_lap_s,
    MAX(CASE WHEN compound_name = 'HARD'   THEN degradation_per_lap END) AS hard_deg_per_lap_s
  FROM stint_data
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, team_name
)
SELECT
  cc.driver_number,
  cc.driver_name,
  cc.team_name,
  ROUND(cc.medium_avg_lap_s::numeric, 3)                          AS medium_avg_lap_s,
  ROUND(cc.hard_avg_lap_s::numeric, 3)                            AS hard_avg_lap_s,
  ROUND((cc.medium_avg_lap_s - cc.hard_avg_lap_s)::numeric, 3)   AS medium_vs_hard_avg_delta_s,
  ROUND(cc.medium_best_lap_s::numeric, 3)                         AS medium_best_lap_s,
  ROUND(cc.hard_best_lap_s::numeric, 3)                           AS hard_best_lap_s,
  ROUND((cc.medium_best_lap_s - cc.hard_best_lap_s)::numeric, 3) AS medium_vs_hard_best_delta_s,
  cc.medium_valid_laps,
  cc.hard_valid_laps,
  cc.medium_first_lap,
  cc.medium_last_lap,
  cc.hard_first_lap,
  cc.hard_last_lap,
  ROUND(cc.medium_deg_per_lap_s::numeric, 4)                      AS medium_deg_per_lap_s,
  ROUND(cc.hard_deg_per_lap_s::numeric, 4)                        AS hard_deg_per_lap_s,
  CASE
    WHEN cc.medium_avg_lap_s IS NULL OR cc.hard_avg_lap_s IS NULL THEN 'insufficient data'
    WHEN cc.medium_avg_lap_s <= cc.hard_avg_lap_s THEN 'MEDIUM faster on avg'
    ELSE 'HARD faster on avg'
  END AS compound_pace_verdict
FROM crossover_calc cc
ORDER BY cc.driver_number

results.question: Across stints 1, 2 and 3 at Bahrain 2025, did Hamilton's middle-stint medium deltas to Leclerc reverse on the hard, and was the late-stint cliff a warmup artefact or thermal degradation?
results.author_note: RaceFans Hamilton middle-stint optimism piece + 'pace dropped over following laps' note.
results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
results.answer: Hamilton's delta to Leclerc did narrow significantly on the second medium stint but did not fully reverse: on Stint 1 (medium) Hamilton was +0.628s slower overall (+0.485s in the settled phase), while on Stint 2 (medium) the gap collapsed to just +0.069s overall (+0.077s settled), so the mediums converged but Hamilton never went faster than Leclerc on that compound. On the Stint 3 hard, Hamilton was actually marginally quicker overall (−0.060s), confirming a genuine reversal on the hard tyre. The 'late-stint cliff' visible in the overall hard-stint averages (avg ~100.7s vs best ~96.2s) is overwhelmingly a warmup artefact: the warmup-phase average for Hamilton was an extraordinary 137.96s with a slope of +10.2s/lap, almost certainly reflecting a slow out-lap or safety-car laps, while the settled hard-tyre phase shows a very modest degradation slope of only +0.080s/lap for Hamilton vs +0.099s/lap for Leclerc — indicating minimal thermal degradation once up to temperature.
results.answerReasoning: Stint 1 summary shows ham_delta_to_lec = +0.628s; Stint 2 summary shows +0.069s — convergence but not reversal on mediums. Stint 3 hard summary shows −0.060s, confirming Hamilton faster on hards. The warmup phase for Stint 3 shows avg lap of 137.96s for Hamilton with a +10.2s/lap slope, which is clearly an anomalous slow lap (pit-out or SC), not representative of tyre degradation. The settled (non-warmup) hard phase shows deg_slope of only +0.080s/lap for Hamilton, ruling out significant thermal degradation as the cause of the high average.
results.generationNotes:
The question asks three things: (1) whether Hamilton's middle-stint (stint 2, medium) pace delta to Leclerc reversed on the hard (stint 3), (2) whether the late-stint cliff is a warmup artefact or thermal degradation. The context pins session_key=10014 (Bahrain 2025) and driver_number=16 (Leclerc); Hamilton is driver 44.

Approach:
- Pull stint-level summaries from core.stint_summary for both drivers across stints 1-3, computing avg_valid_lap, best_valid_lap, degradation_per_lap, and the Hamilton-minus-Leclerc delta per stint.
- Pull lap-level detail from core.laps_enriched for both drivers, splitting each stint into warmup (tyre_age_on_lap ≤ 2) vs non-warmup phases, computing avg lap time, fuel-adjusted avg, and a regression slope (deg_slope) within each phase. This directly answers whether the late-stint cliff is warmup-driven (large warmup avg vs small non-warmup avg) or thermal degradation (high deg_slope in non-warmup phase).
- UNION the two result sets so the analyst sees both the stint-level comparison and the warmup/non-warmup phase breakdown in one result.
- Filter to driver_numbers 44 and 16, session_key 10014, stints 1-3 only.
- No cross-join of telemetry tables; uses only core.stint_summary and core.laps_enriched which are safe for this grain. | session_pin_verified(session_key=10014)
results.sql:
WITH target_drivers AS (
  SELECT driver_number, driver_name, team_name
  FROM core.stint_summary
  WHERE session_key = 10014
    AND driver_number IN (44, 16)
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, team_name
),
stint_data AS (
  SELECT
    ss.driver_number,
    ss.driver_name,
    ss.team_name,
    ss.stint_number,
    ss.compound_name,
    ss.lap_start,
    ss.lap_end,
    ss.stint_length_laps,
    ss.tyre_age_at_start,
    ss.fresh_tyre,
    ss.avg_valid_lap,
    ss.best_valid_lap,
    ss.degradation_per_lap
  FROM core.stint_summary ss
  WHERE ss.session_key = 10014
    AND ss.driver_number IN (44, 16)
    AND ss.stint_number IN (1, 2, 3)
),
lap_detail AS (
  SELECT
    le.driver_number,
    le.driver_name,
    le.stint_number,
    le.compound_name,
    le.lap_number,
    le.tyre_age_on_lap,
    le.lap_duration,
    le.is_valid,
    le.is_pit_out_lap,
    le.fuel_adj_lap_time,
    -- warmup flag: first 2 laps of stint
    CASE WHEN le.tyre_age_on_lap <= 2 THEN TRUE ELSE FALSE END AS is_warmup_lap
  FROM core.laps_enriched le
  WHERE le.session_key = 10014
    AND le.driver_number IN (44, 16)
    AND le.stint_number IN (1, 2, 3)
    AND le.is_valid = TRUE
    AND le.is_pit_lap = FALSE
),
stint_phase_avg AS (
  SELECT
    driver_number,
    driver_name,
    stint_number,
    compound_name,
    is_warmup_lap,
    COUNT(*) AS lap_count,
    ROUND(AVG(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS avg_lap_s,
    ROUND(AVG(fuel_adj_lap_time)::numeric, 3) AS avg_fuel_adj_lap_s,
    ROUND(MIN(lap_duration)::numeric, 3) AS best_lap_s,
    -- degradation proxy: slope of lap_duration over tyre_age within phase
    ROUND(
      REGR_SLOPE(lap_duration, tyre_age_on_lap)::numeric, 4
    ) AS deg_slope_s_per_lap
  FROM lap_detail
  GROUP BY driver_number, driver_name, stint_number, compound_name, is_warmup_lap
),
pivot_ham_lec AS (
  SELECT
    h.stint_number,
    h.compound_name,
    h.is_warmup_lap,
    h.lap_count AS ham_laps,
    h.avg_lap_s AS ham_avg_s,
    h.avg_fuel_adj_lap_s AS ham_fuel_adj_s,
    h.deg_slope_s_per_lap AS ham_deg_slope,
    l.lap_count AS lec_laps,
    l.avg_lap_s AS lec_avg_s,
    l.avg_fuel_adj_lap_s AS lec_fuel_adj_s,
    l.deg_slope_s_per_lap AS lec_deg_slope,
    ROUND((h.avg_lap_s - l.avg_lap_s)::numeric, 3) AS ham_delta_to_lec_s,
    ROUND((h.avg_fuel_adj_lap_s - l.avg_fuel_adj_lap_s)::numeric, 3) AS ham_fuel_adj_delta_to_lec_s
  FROM stint_phase_avg h
  JOIN stint_phase_avg l
    ON h.stint_number = l.stint_number
    AND h.compound_name = l.compound_name
    AND h.is_warmup_lap = l.is_warmup_lap
    AND h.driver_number = 44
    AND l.driver_number = 16
),
stint_summary_compare AS (
  SELECT
    h.stint_number,
    h.compound_name,
    h.lap_start AS ham_lap_start,
    h.lap_end AS ham_lap_end,
    h.stint_length_laps AS ham_stint_laps,
    h.tyre_age_at_start AS ham_tyre_age_start,
    h.fresh_tyre AS ham_fresh,
    ROUND(h.avg_valid_lap::numeric, 3) AS ham_avg_valid_s,
    ROUND(h.best_valid_lap::numeric, 3) AS ham_best_valid_s,
    ROUND(h.degradation_per_lap::numeric, 4) AS ham_deg_per_lap,
    l.lap_start AS lec_lap_start,
    l.lap_end AS lec_lap_end,
    l.stint_length_laps AS lec_stint_laps,
    l.tyre_age_at_start AS lec_tyre_age_start,
    l.fresh_tyre AS lec_fresh,
    ROUND(l.avg_valid_lap::numeric, 3) AS lec_avg_valid_s,
    ROUND(l.best_valid_lap::numeric, 3) AS lec_best_valid_s,
    ROUND(l.degradation_per_lap::numeric, 4) AS lec_deg_per_lap,
    ROUND((h.avg_valid_lap - l.avg_valid_lap)::numeric, 3) AS ham_delta_to_lec_s,
    ROUND((h.degradation_per_lap - l.degradation_per_lap)::numeric, 4) AS ham_deg_delta_to_lec
  FROM stint_data h
  JOIN stint_data l
    ON h.stint_number = l.stint_number
    AND h.compound_name = l.compound_name
    AND h.driver_number = 44
    AND l.driver_number = 16
)
SELECT
  'STINT_SUMMARY' AS analysis_type,
  sc.stint_number,
  sc.compound_name,
  NULL::boolean AS is_warmup_lap,
  sc.ham_lap_start,
  sc.ham_lap_end,
  sc.ham_stint_laps,
  sc.ham_tyre_age_start,
  sc.ham_fresh,
  sc.ham_avg_valid_s,
  sc.ham_best_valid_s,
  sc.ham_deg_per_lap,
  sc.lec_lap_start,
  sc.lec_lap_end,
  sc.lec_stint_laps,
  sc.lec_tyre_age_start,
  sc.lec_fresh,
  sc.lec_avg_valid_s,
  sc.lec_best_valid_s,
  sc.lec_deg_per_lap,
  sc.ham_delta_to_lec_s,
  sc.ham_deg_delta_to_lec,
  NULL::numeric AS ham_phase_avg_s,
  NULL::numeric AS lec_phase_avg_s,
  NULL::numeric AS ham_fuel_adj_s,
  NULL::numeric AS lec_fuel_adj_s,
  NULL::numeric AS ham_phase_deg_slope,
  NULL::numeric AS lec_phase_deg_slope,
  NULL::numeric AS ham_phase_delta_s,
  NULL::numeric AS ham_fuel_adj_delta_s
FROM stint_summary_compare sc

UNION ALL

SELECT
  'LAP_PHASE' AS analysis_type,
  p.stint_number,
  p.compound_name,
  p.is_warmup_lap,
  NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::boolean,
  NULL::numeric, NULL::numeric, NULL::numeric,
  NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::integer, NULL::boolean,
  NULL::numeric, NULL::numeric, NULL::numeric,
  p.ham_delta_to_lec_s,
  NULL::numeric,
  p.ham_avg_s,
  p.lec_avg_s,
  p.ham_fuel_adj_s,
  p.lec_fuel_adj_s,
  p.ham_deg_slope,
  p.lec_deg_slope,
  p.ham_delta_to_lec_s,
  p.ham_fuel_adj_delta_to_lec_s
FROM pivot_ham_lec p

ORDER BY stint_number, analysis_type DESC, is_warmup_lap NULLS FIRST
LIMIT 100

results.question: At Monza 2025, where front-axle graining on the C4 medium was the dominant deg mode, did McLaren's higher-graining stint-1 mediums show a sharper cliff than Ferrari's, after fuel-correcting first 3 vs last 3 laps of each stint?

results.adequacyReason: Answer appears to address the question.
